subject: English Language
title: Language Techniques: Persuasive Writing
passage: Persuasive writing uses a range of techniques to convince the reader. Pathos appeals to the writer's authority or credibility, making the audience trust their argument. Ethos targets the reader's emotions, using feelings like fear, sympathy or excitement to win them over. Logos uses logical reasoning and evidence to build an argument. The 'rule of three' groups three words or phrases together for emphasis and rhythm. Anaphora involves the repetition of words at the beginning of successive clauses. Writers also use rhetorical questions to engage the reader directly, and hyperbole to exaggerate for effect.

hallucinations.text: Pathos appeals to the writer's authority or credibility, making the audience trust their argument. Ethos targets the reader's emotions
hallucinations.explanation: These two are the wrong way round. Ethos is the appeal to the writer's authority and credibility — it builds trust. Pathos is the appeal to the reader's emotions — it targets feelings like fear or sympathy. The definitions have been swapped.

subject: English Language
title: Reading Non-Fiction: Newspaper Articles
passage: Newspaper articles follow specific structural conventions. A broadsheet newspaper typically presents more detailed analysis and longer articles, while a tabloid focuses on shorter, more sensational stories. The headline grabs attention and summarises the story, while the standfirst — a brief introductory paragraph — provides context. The inverted pyramid structure places the least important background detail first, with the most significant facts saved for the end of the article so that readers have to read the whole piece. Sub-headings help readers navigate longer pieces. Feature articles may take a more chronological or narrative approach.

hallucinations.text: The inverted pyramid structure places the least important background detail first, with the most significant facts saved for the end of the article
hallucinations.explanation: This is the opposite of how the inverted pyramid works. The inverted pyramid places the most important information first — the who, what, where, when, and why — so readers get the key facts immediately. Less important detail comes later. This is why editors can cut the end of an article for space without losing the story.

subject: English Language
title: Spoken Language: Dialect and Accent
passage: Spoken language varies significantly across regions and social groups. An accent refers to a variety of language characterised by its vocabulary and grammar, while a dialect refers only to pronunciation. Received Pronunciation (RP), sometimes called 'the Queen's English', is spoken by a small minority of the UK population and is often associated with prestige and formal contexts. Sociolinguist William Labov conducted a famous study on the relationship between social class and language variation in New York City department stores, finding that workers in higher-end stores were more likely to use prestige forms. Standard English is the variety taught in schools and used in formal writing.

hallucinations.text: An accent refers to a variety of language characterised by its vocabulary and grammar, while a dialect refers only to pronunciation
hallucinations.explanation: These definitions are the wrong way round. A dialect refers to a variety of language that differs in vocabulary and grammar as well as pronunciation. An accent refers only to pronunciation — the way words sound, without any difference in grammar or vocabulary. For example, two people can have different accents but speak the same dialect.

subject: English Literature
title: Shakespeare: Macbeth
passage: Macbeth, written by William Shakespeare around 1606, is a tragedy exploring themes of ambition, guilt, and the corrupting nature of power. The play was written to appeal to King James I of England, who had a keen interest in witchcraft. Lady Macbeth is often considered the more ruthlessly ambitious of the two central characters, famously calling on spirits to 'unsex me here' and fill her with cruelty. The three witches, or Weird Sisters, represent order and divine justice in the play, rewarding the righteous and punishing wrongdoers. Macbeth is one of Shakespeare's shortest tragedies. The theme of guilt is explored through Macbeth's hallucinations — including the dagger and Banquo's ghost — and Lady Macbeth's sleepwalking.

hallucinations.text: The three witches, or Weird Sisters, represent order and divine justice in the play, rewarding the righteous and punishing wrongdoers
hallucinations.explanation: The witches represent chaos, fate, and the supernatural — not order or divine justice. They do not reward or punish according to any moral code; instead they tempt and manipulate. Their prophecies trigger Macbeth's ambition but they do not dispense justice. Order in the play is associated with the natural hierarchy, which the witches help to disrupt.

subject: English Literature
title: Poetry: War Poetry
passage: War poetry became particularly prominent during and after the First World War. Wilfred Owen wrote some of the most powerful war poems of the twentieth century. His poem 'Dulce et Decorum Est' celebrates the heroism and patriotism of soldiers, arguing that dying for one's country is a glorious and honourable act. The title — taken from a Latin phrase meaning 'it is sweet and fitting to die for one's country' — expresses Owen's genuine belief in the nobility of sacrifice. Owen was killed in action on 4 November 1918, just one week before the Armistice. Siegfried Sassoon, Owen's close friend and mentor, survived the war. Rupert Brooke, by contrast, wrote romantically about the war in his poem 'The Soldier'.

hallucinations.text: His poem 'Dulce et Decorum Est' celebrates the heroism and patriotism of soldiers, arguing that dying for one's country is a glorious and honourable act. The title — taken from a Latin phrase meaning 'it is sweet and fitting to die for one's country' — expresses Owen's genuine belief in the nobility of sacrifice
hallucinations.explanation: 'Dulce et Decorum Est' does the exact opposite of celebrating heroic death. Owen uses a graphic description of a gas attack to challenge and expose the idea that dying for one's country is noble. The Latin title is used ironically — Owen calls it 'the old lie'. The poem is one of the most powerful anti-war statements in English literature.

subject: English Literature
title: The Novel: 19th Century Fiction
passage: Nineteenth-century novels often grappled with social injustice and class inequality. Charles Dickens published Great Expectations in 1861, using the story of Pip's journey from poverty to wealth to critique the Victorian class system. Dickens originally wrote the novel with an unhappy ending in which Pip and Estella part permanently; he changed it to a more ambiguous ending after advice from a fellow novelist. Mary Shelley published Frankenstein in 1818, making it one of the earliest works of science fiction. The novel was written following a ghost story competition at the Villa Diodati in Switzerland in the summer of 1816, organised and hosted by the poet William Wordsworth.

hallucinations.text: organised and hosted by the poet William Wordsworth
hallucinations.explanation: The famous ghost story gathering at Villa Diodati was hosted by the Romantic poet Lord Byron, not William Wordsworth. The group included Mary Godwin (later Mary Shelley), Percy Bysshe Shelley, Lord Byron, and Byron's physician John Polidori. Wordsworth was not present.

subject: Mathematics
title: Pythagoras' Theorem
passage: Pythagoras' theorem states that in a right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides, expressed as a² + b² = c². The hypotenuse is always the longest side and is opposite the right angle. The theorem can be used to find missing sides in any triangle, as long as you know the length of the other two sides. Evidence of the relationship was known to the Babylonians over 1,000 years before Pythagoras, as demonstrated by the clay tablet Plimpton 322, which contains Pythagorean triples. A Pythagorean triple is a set of three whole numbers that satisfy the theorem, such as 3, 4, and 5.

hallucinations.text: The theorem can be used to find missing sides in any triangle, as long as you know the length of the other two sides
hallucinations.explanation: Pythagoras' theorem only works in right-angled triangles — triangles that contain a 90° angle. It cannot be used on any triangle. For triangles without a right angle, you would need to use the sine rule or cosine rule instead.

subject: Mathematics
title: Statistics: Types of Data
passage: In statistics, data can be classified in several ways. Quantitative data is numerical, whereas qualitative data is descriptive or categorical. Quantitative data is further divided into discrete data — which can take any value within a range, such as height or temperature — and continuous data, which can only take certain specific values, such as the number of students in a class. Primary data is collected first-hand by the researcher, while secondary data has been collected by someone else. A frequency table is used to organise data into groups, and a histogram is used to display continuous data. When collecting data, bias should be avoided by using a representative sample.

hallucinations.text: discrete data — which can take any value within a range, such as height or temperature — and continuous data, which can only take certain specific values, such as the number of students in a class
hallucinations.explanation: These definitions are the wrong way round. Continuous data can take any value within a range — such as height, weight, or temperature. Discrete data can only take specific, separate values — such as the number of students in a class (you can't have 12.7 students). The examples given have been attached to the wrong type.

subject: Mathematics
title: Algebra: Quadratic Equations
passage: A quadratic equation is one in which the highest power of the variable is 2, in the form ax² + bx + c = 0. Quadratic equations can be solved by factorising, completing the square, or using the quadratic formula: x = (−b ± √(b²−4ac)) / 2a. The discriminant, b²−4ac, determines the nature of the roots: if it is positive, there are two distinct real roots; if zero, there is one repeated root; if negative, there are two distinct real roots. When solving quadratic equations by factorising, we look for two numbers that multiply to give 'ac' and add to give 'b'. The quadratic formula always works, even when factorising is difficult or impossible.

hallucinations.text: if negative, there are two distinct real roots
hallucinations.explanation: When the discriminant (b²−4ac) is negative, there are no real roots — the equation has no real solutions. A negative number has no real square root, so the quadratic formula cannot give a real answer. There are two distinct real roots only when the discriminant is positive.

subject: Biology
title: Cell Biology: Mitosis and Meiosis
passage: Cell division in humans occurs through two main processes: mitosis and meiosis. Mitosis produces four genetically unique haploid cells and is used to produce gametes — sperm and egg cells. During mitosis, homologous chromosomes exchange genetic material in a process called crossing over, which increases genetic variation. Meiosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells and is used for growth and repair of body tissues. DNA replication occurs before cell division begins, during a phase called interphase. Both processes begin with a single cell and end with new cells that carry the organism's genetic information.

hallucinations.text: Mitosis produces four genetically unique haploid cells and is used to produce gametes — sperm and egg cells. During mitosis, homologous chromosomes exchange genetic material in a process called crossing over, which increases genetic variation. Meiosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells and is used for growth and repair of body tissues
hallucinations.explanation: The descriptions of mitosis and meiosis have been swapped. Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells and is used for growth and repair. Meiosis produces four genetically unique haploid cells, used to make gametes (sperm and egg cells) — it involves crossing over, which creates genetic variation.

subject: Biology
title: Genetics: DNA and Inheritance
passage: DNA, or deoxyribonucleic acid, carries the genetic instructions for the development and functioning of all living organisms. The double helix structure of DNA was discovered in 1953 by James Watson and Francis Crick, based significantly on X-ray diffraction data collected by Rosalind Franklin. A gene is a sequence of DNA that codes for a specific protein. Gregor Mendel, an Austrian friar, conducted experiments on pea plants in the 1850s and 1860s, establishing the laws of inheritance. His work was largely ignored during his lifetime but was rediscovered by other scientists around 1900. Alleles are alternative versions of a gene. A recessive allele is always expressed if present, regardless of what the other allele is.

hallucinations.text: A recessive allele is always expressed if present, regardless of what the other allele is
hallucinations.explanation: This describes a dominant allele, not a recessive one. A dominant allele is always expressed when present — even if paired with a different allele. A recessive allele is only expressed when two copies are present (homozygous recessive). If a dominant allele is present alongside a recessive one, the dominant allele's characteristic will show.

subject: Biology
title: Ecology: Food Webs and Energy Transfer
passage: In ecosystems, energy flows from producers to consumers through food chains and food webs. Producers, such as plants and algae, convert sunlight into chemical energy through photosynthesis. Primary consumers eat producers; secondary consumers eat primary consumers. At each trophic level, approximately 90% of energy is transferred to the next level — with only around 10% lost as heat through respiration and in waste. This means that food chains rarely have more than four or five trophic levels. The biomass pyramid always has a wider base than top, as there is always less biomass at higher trophic levels. Decomposers, such as bacteria and fungi, break down dead organic matter and return nutrients to the soil.

hallucinations.text: approximately 90% of energy is transferred to the next level — with only around 10% lost as heat through respiration and in waste
hallucinations.explanation: This is the wrong way round. Only approximately 10% of energy is transferred to the next trophic level — around 90% is lost as heat through respiration, movement, and in waste products. This inefficiency is why food chains are short and why there is less biomass at higher levels.

subject: Chemistry
title: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table
passage: The modern understanding of atomic structure describes the atom as consisting of a central nucleus containing protons and electrons, while neutrons orbit the nucleus in shells. The periodic table organises elements by increasing atomic number. Dmitri Mendeleev published his first periodic table in 1869, arranging elements by atomic mass and leaving gaps for undiscovered elements. He predicted the properties of several undiscovered elements, including gallium and germanium, which were later found to match his predictions closely. Ernest Rutherford discovered the atomic nucleus in 1911 through his gold foil experiment, in which he fired alpha particles at a thin gold sheet and found that some were deflected back, suggesting a dense central core.

hallucinations.text: a central nucleus containing protons and electrons, while neutrons orbit the nucleus in shells
hallucinations.explanation: The subatomic particles are in the wrong places. The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons orbit the nucleus in shells (energy levels). Electrons are negatively charged and much lighter than protons or neutrons. This is a fundamental part of atomic structure.

subject: Chemistry
title: Chemical Bonding
passage: Chemical bonds form when atoms interact to achieve a more stable electron configuration. Ionic bonding involves the sharing of electron pairs between non-metal atoms. Covalent bonding involves the transfer of electrons from a metal to a non-metal, creating oppositely charged ions that attract each other. Metallic bonding involves a lattice of positive metal ions surrounded by a sea of delocalised electrons, which explains the conductivity and malleability of metals. Hydrogen bonding is a particularly strong type of intermolecular force that occurs between molecules containing hydrogen bonded to nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine. Water's unusually high boiling point compared to other small molecules is explained by the network of hydrogen bonds between water molecules.

hallucinations.text: Ionic bonding involves the sharing of electron pairs between non-metal atoms. Covalent bonding involves the transfer of electrons from a metal to a non-metal, creating oppositely charged ions that attract each other
hallucinations.explanation: The definitions of ionic and covalent bonding have been swapped. Ionic bonding involves the transfer of electrons from a metal to a non-metal, forming oppositely charged ions that attract each other. Covalent bonding involves the sharing of electron pairs between non-metal atoms.

subject: Chemistry
title: Rates of Reaction
passage: The rate of a chemical reaction is affected by several factors: temperature, concentration, surface area, the presence of a catalyst, and pressure (for gases). Increasing temperature speeds up a reaction because particles have more kinetic energy and collide more frequently and with greater energy. Collision theory states that reactions occur when particles collide with sufficient energy — this minimum energy is called the activation energy. A catalyst speeds up a reaction by providing an alternative pathway with a higher activation energy, meaning fewer particles need to collide for the reaction to proceed. Enzymes are biological catalysts made of protein. The rate of a reaction can be measured by tracking how quickly a reactant is used up or a product is formed.

hallucinations.text: A catalyst speeds up a reaction by providing an alternative pathway with a higher activation energy, meaning fewer particles need to collide for the reaction to proceed
hallucinations.explanation: A catalyst provides a pathway with a lower activation energy, not higher. Because the activation energy is lower, more particles have enough energy to react, so the reaction happens faster. A higher activation energy would make the reaction slower, not faster — that would be the opposite of what a catalyst does.

subject: Physics
title: Forces and Newton's Laws
passage: Newton's three laws of motion describe the relationship between a body and the forces acting on it. The first law states that an object will continue to accelerate in the direction of motion unless a force acts to slow it down or change its direction. The second law states that force equals mass times acceleration (F = ma). The third law states that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. Isaac Newton published these laws in his 1687 work Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica. Newton developed many of his key ideas while staying at his family home in Woolsthorpe during the 1665 plague, when Cambridge University was closed.

hallucinations.text: The first law states that an object will continue to accelerate in the direction of motion unless a force acts to slow it down or change its direction
hallucinations.explanation: Newton's First Law states that an object will remain at rest or continue moving at a constant velocity (same speed, same direction) unless acted on by an unbalanced force. It does not say objects naturally accelerate — that idea belongs to the Second Law. An object moving at constant speed in a straight line has no net force acting on it.

subject: Physics
title: Electricity: Circuits and Ohm's Law
passage: Electric current is the flow of charge through a conductor. In a series circuit, components are connected in a single loop; the current is the same throughout but voltage is shared. In a parallel circuit, components are connected across the same two points; the voltage is the same across each component but current is shared. Ohm's Law states that the current through a conductor is directly proportional to the voltage across it, provided temperature remains constant: V = IR. Ohm's Law only applies to ohmic conductors; non-ohmic conductors such as diodes do not follow this relationship. Resistors in series add directly: R_total = R₁ + R₂.

subject: Physics
title: Waves: Properties and Electromagnetic Spectrum
passage: Waves transfer energy without transferring matter. Transverse waves oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel (e.g. light, water waves), while longitudinal waves oscillate parallel to the direction of travel (e.g. sound). The electromagnetic spectrum, in order of decreasing frequency, runs from: radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays. All electromagnetic waves travel at the speed of light in a vacuum, approximately 3 × 10⁸ m/s. The wave equation is v = fλ, where v is wave speed, f is frequency, and λ is wavelength. Higher frequency electromagnetic waves carry more energy, which is why gamma rays are the most dangerous and radio waves the least.

hallucinations.text: The electromagnetic spectrum, in order of decreasing frequency, runs from: radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays
hallucinations.explanation: The list is in order of increasing frequency, not decreasing. Radio waves have the lowest frequency (and longest wavelength); gamma rays have the highest frequency (and shortest wavelength). Describing the list as 'decreasing frequency' directly contradicts what the list shows — gamma rays having the most energy confirms they have the highest frequency.

subject: History
title: Causes of the First World War
passage: The First World War broke out in August 1914 and involved most of the world's great powers. Historians often cite four main causes, summarised by the acronym MAIN: Militarism, Alliance systems, Imperialism, and Nationalism. The immediate trigger was the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary in Sarajevo on 28 June 1914. He was shot by Gavrilo Princip, a Bosnian Serb nationalist with ties to the Black Hand secret society. Germany's plan for a two-front war was set out in the Schlieffen Plan, which called for a rapid defeat of Russia in the east before turning to face France in the west, to avoid fighting both countries simultaneously.

hallucinations.text: the Schlieffen Plan, which called for a rapid defeat of Russia in the east before turning to face France in the west
hallucinations.explanation: The Schlieffen Plan was the other way round. It called for a rapid defeat of France in the west first — by sweeping through Belgium — before turning east to face Russia. The plan relied on Russia being slow to mobilise, giving Germany time to knock out France quickly and then shift forces east. Getting this wrong inverts the entire logic of the plan.

subject: History
title: The Cold War: Origins and Key Events
passage: The Cold War was a period of geopolitical tension between the United States and the Soviet Union that lasted roughly from 1947 to 1991. Following the Second World War, ideological differences between Western capitalism and Soviet communism led to an arms race and a series of proxy conflicts. The Truman Doctrine of 1947 committed the US to supporting nations threatened by Soviet expansion. The Berlin Wall was built in August 1961 to prevent East Germans from fleeing to the West, and was demolished on 9 November 1989. The Cuban Missile Crisis of October 1962 brought the world close to nuclear war after US spy planes discovered Soviet nuclear missiles being installed in Cuba, directed by Soviet Premier Nikita Khrushchev and approved personally by President John F. Kennedy.

hallucinations.text: directed by Soviet Premier Nikita Khrushchev and approved personally by President John F. Kennedy
hallucinations.explanation: Kennedy did not approve the installation of Soviet missiles — he ordered their removal. It was Khrushchev who ordered the missiles to be placed in Cuba. Kennedy's response was to impose a naval blockade and demand the missiles be withdrawn. The passage implies Kennedy was on the Soviet side, which is completely wrong.

subject: History
title: The British Empire and Decolonisation
passage: At its height in the early 20th century, the British Empire covered approximately a quarter of the world's land surface and governed around 400 million people. India, often referred to as the 'jewel in the crown' of the Empire, gained independence on 15 August 1947 following a prolonged independence movement led significantly by Mahatma Gandhi and his policy of non-violent civil disobedience. The partition of India and Pakistan led to one of the largest mass migrations in human history, with an estimated 10–20 million people displaced. Ghana became the first sub-Saharan African country to gain independence from France in 1957, under the leadership of Kwame Nkrumah. The handover of Hong Kong to China took place on 1 July 1997.

hallucinations.text: Ghana became the first sub-Saharan African country to gain independence from France in 1957
hallucinations.explanation: Ghana gained independence from Britain, not France. Ghana was formerly the British colony of the Gold Coast. France had its own African colonies (French West Africa, French Equatorial Africa, etc.), which gained independence mainly in 1960. Ghana's independence from Britain in 1957 was a landmark moment in African decolonisation.

subject: Geography
title: Tectonic Hazards: Earthquakes and Volcanoes
passage: The Earth's crust is divided into tectonic plates that move due to convection currents in the mantle. At divergent boundaries, two plates move towards each other; the denser oceanic plate is subducted beneath the lighter continental plate, which can cause earthquakes and volcanoes. At convergent boundaries, plates move apart, allowing magma to rise up through the gap and form new crust on the ocean floor. At transform boundaries, plates slide past each other horizontally, causing earthquakes but not volcanic activity. The Pacific Ring of Fire is a region of intense seismic and volcanic activity that encircles the Pacific Ocean, accounting for approximately 90% of the world's earthquakes.

hallucinations.text: At divergent boundaries, two plates move towards each other; the denser oceanic plate is subducted beneath the lighter continental plate, which can cause earthquakes and volcanoes. At convergent boundaries, plates move apart, allowing magma to rise up through the gap and form new crust on the ocean floor
hallucinations.explanation: The definitions of divergent and convergent boundaries have been swapped. At convergent (destructive) boundaries, plates move towards each other and the oceanic plate is subducted. At divergent (constructive) boundaries, plates move apart, magma rises, and new crust is formed. Getting these the wrong way round is a fundamental error in understanding plate tectonics.

subject: Geography
title: Urban Issues: Urbanisation and Megacities
passage: Urbanisation is the process by which an increasing proportion of a country's population lives in urban areas. It is driven by rural-to-urban migration as people seek employment, education, and improved living standards. A megacity is generally defined as an urban area with a population of over 10 million. As of 2023, there are over 35 megacities worldwide, with the largest being Tokyo, Japan, with a metropolitan population of approximately 37 million. Rapid urbanisation in low-income countries often leads to the growth of informal settlements, known as slums or shanty towns. Lagos, Nigeria, is one of the fastest-growing cities in the world and is projected to become the world's largest city by 2100.

subject: Geography
title: Climate Change: Causes and Effects
passage: Climate change refers to long-term shifts in global temperatures and weather patterns. While natural factors have always caused climate variability, since the mid-20th century human activities have been the dominant cause of observed climate change, primarily through the emission of greenhouse gases. Carbon dioxide (CO₂) is the most significant anthropogenic greenhouse gas, released through the burning of fossil fuels and deforestation. The Paris Agreement, signed in 2015, aims to limit global warming to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. The IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change) was established in 1988 by the United Nations Environment Programme and the World Health Organization. Rising sea levels threaten low-lying coastal regions and island nations such as the Maldives and Tuvalu.

hallucinations.text: established in 1988 by the United Nations Environment Programme and the World Health Organization
hallucinations.explanation: The IPCC was established by the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) — not the World Health Organization (WHO). The WMO deals with weather and climate; the WHO deals with public health. These are two different UN bodies with very different roles.

subject: French
title: La Francophonie
passage: French is spoken as an official language in 29 countries worldwide and is used by approximately 300 million people across five continents. Countries where French is an official language are collectively referred to as 'La Francophonie'. French is the official language of several international organisations including the United Nations and the European Union. The Académie française, founded in 1635 by Cardinal Richelieu under King Louis XIII, is responsible for regulating the French language. French was the dominant language of European diplomacy from the 17th century until the Second World War, when it was gradually replaced by English.

hallucinations.text: French was the dominant language of European diplomacy from the 17th century until the Second World War, when it was gradually replaced by English
hallucinations.explanation: French's dominance in European diplomacy began to decline after the First World War, not the Second. The Treaty of Versailles in 1919 was one of the first major international agreements drafted in both French and English — marking the shift. By the end of the First World War, English had begun to challenge French as the leading international language of diplomacy.

subject: French
title: French Grammar: The Subjunctive
passage: The subjunctive mood in French is used to express doubt, uncertainty, desire, emotion, and necessity. It is typically introduced by certain conjunctions and by verbs of wanting, feeling, or doubting, followed by 'que'. For example, 'Il faut que tu viennes' means 'It is necessary that you come'. The subjunctive has four tenses in French: present, imperfect, perfect, and pluperfect, though in modern spoken French only the present and perfect subjunctive are commonly used. French has two grammatical genders — masculine and feminine — unlike German, which has three genders including neuter. The subjunctive is also used after expressions of doubt such as 'bien que' (although), 'pour que' (so that), and 'à moins que' (unless).

subject: French
title: French Culture: History and Traditions
passage: France is the most visited country in the world, receiving over 90 million international tourists annually. The French Republic was proclaimed on 21 September 1792, during the Revolution, and the country has since operated under five distinct republics. Bastille Day, celebrated on 14 July, commemorates the storming of the Bastille prison in 1789, which marked a turning point in the French Revolution. The Eiffel Tower, constructed for the 1889 World's Fair, was designed by Gustave Eiffel as a permanent landmark and symbol of French achievement that was always intended to remain in place as a tribute to French engineering.

hallucinations.text: designed by Gustave Eiffel as a permanent landmark and symbol of French achievement that was always intended to remain in place as a tribute to French engineering
hallucinations.explanation: The Eiffel Tower was designed as a temporary structure. It was built for the 1889 World's Fair and was originally supposed to be dismantled 20 years later. It was only kept because it proved valuable as a radio transmission tower. The idea that it was 'always intended to remain' is false — its survival was an accident of utility, not the original plan.

subject: Spanish
title: Spanish-Speaking Countries
passage: Spanish is the official language of 20 countries and is spoken by over 500 million people as a native language, making it the world's second most spoken language by native speakers after Mandarin Chinese. The Spanish-speaking world spans Europe, the Americas, and Equatorial Guinea in Africa. In Latin America, Spanish arrived following the Spanish conquest in the 15th and 16th centuries. The Royal Spanish Academy (Real Academia Española), founded in 1713, is responsible for regulating the Spanish language. Brazil is the only country in South America where Spanish is not an official language; Portuguese is spoken there instead.

hallucinations.text: Brazil is the only country in South America where Spanish is not an official language
hallucinations.explanation: Brazil is not the only such country. Guyana's official language is English, Suriname's is Dutch, and French Guiana (an overseas territory of France) uses French. None of these have Spanish as an official language. Brazil is simply the largest and most well-known non-Spanish-speaking country in South America.

subject: Spanish
title: Spanish Grammar: Ser vs Estar
passage: One of the key challenges for English speakers learning Spanish is the distinction between the two verbs meaning 'to be': ser and estar. Ser is used for permanent or inherent characteristics such as nationality, origin, profession, and personality. Estar is used for temporary states, locations, and conditions. For example, 'Soy inglés' (I am English) uses ser, while 'Estoy cansado' (I am tired) uses estar. However, the distinction is not always straightforward — location of events uses ser ('La boda es en Madrid'), while location of people and objects uses estar. Spanish also has two past tenses in common use: the preterite, for completed past actions, and the imperfect, for ongoing or habitual past states.

subject: Spanish
title: Spanish Culture: Festivals and Traditions
passage: Spain is renowned for its rich cultural traditions and festivals. La Tomatina, held annually in Buñol, Valencia, on the last Wednesday of August, is a festival in which participants throw tomatoes at each other. The Running of the Bulls (el encierro) in Pamplona is part of the San Fermín festival held each July. Flamenco, a traditional art form combining song, guitar, and dance, originates from the Castile region of northern Spain and was added to the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2010. It is one of Spain's most recognisable cultural exports, performed in tablaos (flamenco venues) across the country.

hallucinations.text: Flamenco, a traditional art form combining song, guitar, and dance, originates from the Castile region of northern Spain
hallucinations.explanation: Flamenco originates from Andalusia in southern Spain — not Castile in the north. Andalusia (the region containing cities such as Seville, Granada, and Córdoba) is the heartland of flamenco culture. Castile is associated with very different traditions. Getting the region wrong misplaces flamenco by the full length of the country.

subject: German
title: German-Speaking Countries
passage: German is the official language of Germany, Austria, and one of the official languages of Switzerland, Liechtenstein, Luxembourg, and Belgium. It is the most widely spoken native language in the European Union, with approximately 95 million native speakers. Standard German (Hochdeutsch) is taught in schools and used in formal contexts, while numerous regional dialects exist across German-speaking areas. The German language belongs to the North Germanic branch of the Indo-European language family, along with languages such as Swedish, Norwegian, and Danish. Martin Luther's translation of the Bible into German in 1522 played a significant role in standardising the German language.

hallucinations.text: The German language belongs to the North Germanic branch of the Indo-European language family, along with languages such as Swedish, Norwegian, and Danish
hallucinations.explanation: German belongs to the West Germanic branch, not North Germanic. North Germanic (also called Nordic or Scandinavian) includes Swedish, Norwegian, Danish, and Icelandic. West Germanic includes German, English, Dutch, and Afrikaans. English and German are in the same branch — North Germanic is a separate group altogether.

subject: German
title: German Grammar: Cases
passage: German uses four grammatical cases: nominative, accusative, dative, and genitive. The nominative case is used for the subject of a sentence (the person or thing doing the action). The accusative case is used for the direct object (the person or thing receiving the action). The dative case is used for the indirect object (the recipient of the action). The genitive case indicates possession. Articles, adjectives, and pronouns all change their forms depending on the case and the grammatical gender of the noun. German has three grammatical genders: masculine (der), feminine (die), and neuter (das). The plural form of all German nouns uses the definite article 'die', regardless of their gender in the singular.

subject: German
title: German History: The Berlin Wall
passage: The Berlin Wall was erected on 13 August 1961, dividing the city of Berlin into East and West sectors. It was built on the orders of the East German leadership, with Soviet backing, primarily to stop the mass emigration of East Germans to the West — over 3.5 million had left since 1949. The Wall was 155 kilometres long in total and was heavily guarded with watchtowers, searchlights, and a 'death strip'. American President John F. Kennedy visited West Berlin in June 1963 and delivered his famous speech containing the words 'Ich bin ein Berliner'. The Wall fell on the evening of 9 November 1989 after an East German spokesman mistakenly announced that citizens could cross the border immediately.

subject: Religious Studies
title: Christianity: Key Beliefs
passage: Christianity is the world's largest religion, with approximately 2.4 billion followers worldwide. Christians believe in the Trinity: the Father, the Son (Jesus Christ), and the Holy Spirit. The Nicene Creed, first formulated at the Council of Nicaea in 325 AD, is one of the most widely used statements of Christian belief. Jesus Christ is believed by Christians to have been crucified under the Roman governor Pontius Pilate and to have risen from the dead on the third day. The Bible consists of two parts: the Old Testament, which is shared with Judaism, and the New Testament, which describes the life and teachings of Jesus. The New Testament was originally written in Aramaic, the language spoken by Jesus and his disciples in first-century Palestine.

hallucinations.text: The New Testament was originally written in Aramaic, the language spoken by Jesus and his disciples in first-century Palestine
hallucinations.explanation: The New Testament was written in Koine Greek — the common language of the Eastern Mediterranean world in the first century AD. While Jesus and his disciples likely spoke Aramaic in daily life, the texts of the New Testament were composed in Greek so they could be read widely across the Roman Empire.

subject: Religious Studies
title: Islam: Key Beliefs and Practices
passage: Islam is the world's second largest religion, with approximately 1.8 billion followers. Muslims believe in one God (Allah) and that Muhammad is the final prophet. The Five Pillars of Islam are: Shahada (declaration of faith), Salah (prayer five times daily), Zakat (charitable giving), Sawm (fasting during Ramadan), and Hajj (pilgrimage to Mecca). The Qur'an, the holy book of Islam, was revealed to the Prophet Muhammad over 23 years through the angel Jibril (Gabriel) beginning in 610 CE. The Islamic calendar is a lunar calendar and the year of the Hijra (Muhammad's migration from Mecca to Medina) in 622 CE marks the beginning of the Islamic calendar year 1. The two main branches of Islam are Sunni and Shia, the split originating from a dispute over Muhammad's successor after his death in 632 CE.

subject: Religious Studies
title: Ethics: Medical Ethics
passage: Medical ethics involves applying moral principles to medical practice and research. The four principles of medical ethics — autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice — were introduced by bioethicists Tom Beauchamp and James Childress in their 1979 book Principles of Biomedical Ethics. Euthanasia refers to the deliberate ending of a life to relieve suffering. Active euthanasia involves a direct action to end life, while passive euthanasia involves withdrawing treatment. Abortion — the deliberate termination of a pregnancy — was legalised in England, Wales, and Scotland through the Abortion Act 1967. The Catholic Church teaches that life begins at conception and opposes both euthanasia and abortion. Sanctity of life is the belief that human life is sacred and God-given.

subject: Computing
title: Binary and Data Representation
passage: Computers store and process all data as binary — sequences of 0s and 1s. A single binary digit is called a bit; 8 bits make a byte. Binary uses a base-2 number system, meaning each digit position represents a power of 2. To convert the binary number 1101 to decimal: (1×8) + (1×4) + (0×2) + (1×1) = 13. In binary, the leftmost digit always has the lowest value, while the rightmost digit represents the highest power of 2. Text is represented in computers using character encoding standards. ASCII uses 7 bits to represent 128 characters, covering letters, digits, and punctuation. Unicode extended this to include characters from all world languages, using more bits per character.

hallucinations.text: In binary, the leftmost digit always has the lowest value, while the rightmost digit represents the highest power of 2
hallucinations.explanation: This is the wrong way round. In binary, the leftmost digit has the highest value — it represents the largest power of 2. In the example given, the leftmost 1 represents 8 (2³). The rightmost digit has the lowest value (2⁰ = 1). This is the same as in decimal, where the leftmost digit is the most significant.

subject: Computing
title: Algorithms and Programming
passage: An algorithm is a precise set of instructions for solving a problem. Algorithms can be represented using pseudocode, flowcharts, or written descriptions. Key programming concepts include variables (named storage locations), loops (for repetition), conditionals (for decision-making), and functions (reusable blocks of code). Sorting algorithms are commonly studied in computer science. Bubble sort repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps them if in the wrong order; it has a worst-case time complexity of O(n²). Merge sort uses a divide-and-conquer approach and has a time complexity of O(n log n). The concept of an algorithm predates computers; the term derives from the name of the 9th-century Persian mathematician Al-Khwarizmi, whose Latinised name gave us the word.

subject: Computing
title: Networks and Cybersecurity
passage: A computer network allows devices to communicate and share resources. The internet is a global network of networks using the TCP/IP protocol suite. A LAN (Local Area Network) covers a small geographical area, such as a school building, while a WAN (Wide Area Network) spans larger areas. The World Wide Web, invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989 while working at CERN in Geneva, is a system of interlinked documents accessed via the internet using HTTP. Cybersecurity threats include malware, phishing, and denial-of-service attacks. Encryption protects data by converting it into an unreadable format using a key; HTTPS uses TLS encryption to secure web traffic.

subject: Design & Technology
title: Materials: Properties and Selection
passage: The selection of materials in design is based on their physical and mechanical properties. Ferrous metals contain iron and are generally magnetic; examples include mild steel and cast iron. Non-ferrous metals do not contain iron; examples include aluminium, copper, and titanium. Polymers (plastics) are classified as thermosetting plastics (which soften when heated and can be reshaped into new forms) or thermoplastics (which set permanently when moulded and cannot be remelted or reshaped). High-density polyethylene (HDPE) is widely used in bottles and containers. Carbon fibre is an advanced composite material made from thin fibres of carbon woven into fabric and set in a resin matrix; it is extremely strong and light, making it popular in aerospace and high-performance sports equipment.

hallucinations.text: thermosetting plastics (which soften when heated and can be reshaped into new forms) or thermoplastics (which set permanently when moulded and cannot be remelted or reshaped)
hallucinations.explanation: The descriptions have been swapped. Thermoplastics are the ones that soften when heated and can be reshaped — the prefix 'thermo' (heat) gives a clue. Thermosetting plastics set permanently during manufacture and cannot be remelted or reshaped. Examples of thermoplastics include PET and HDPE; thermosetting plastics include epoxy resin and melamine.

subject: Design & Technology
title: Manufacturing Processes
passage: Manufacturing processes transform raw materials into finished products. Casting involves pouring molten material into a mould and allowing it to solidify. Injection moulding is a high-volume process for producing plastic components: molten plastic is forced into a sealed mould under pressure. Laser cutting uses a focused beam of light to cut or engrave materials with high precision. 3D printing, or additive manufacturing, builds objects layer by layer from a digital file; the most common consumer method is Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM), which extrudes thermoplastic filament. The first commercially available 3D printer was developed by Chuck Hull, who invented stereolithography and founded 3D Systems Corporation in 1986.

subject: Design & Technology
title: Sustainability in Design
passage: Sustainable design aims to minimise environmental impact throughout a product's lifecycle. The six Rs of sustainability are: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Refuse, Rethink, and Repair. Lifecycle assessment (LCA) evaluates the environmental impact of a product from raw material extraction through manufacture, use, and end-of-life disposal. Planned obsolescence is the deliberate design of products to become outdated or to fail within a short period, encouraging consumers to purchase replacements. The practice was formalised in the consumer electronics industry in 1924 through the Phoebus Cartel, an agreement between major lightbulb manufacturers including Osram, Philips, and General Electric to limit bulb lifespan to 1,000 hours.

subject: Art & Design
title: Art Movements: Impressionism and Post-Impressionism
passage: Impressionism emerged in France in the 1860s–1870s as a reaction against the formal traditions of academic painting. Impressionist painters sought to capture the fleeting effects of light and atmosphere, often painting outdoors (en plein air). Key impressionist painters include Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, Pierre-Auguste Renoir, and Camille Pissarro. The name 'Impressionism' was derived mockingly from Monet's 1872 painting Impression, Sunrise, following its exhibition at the First Impressionist Exhibition held at the studio of photographer Nadar in Paris in 1874. Post-Impressionism refers to the diverse work of painters including Paul Cézanne, Vincent van Gogh, and Paul Gauguin. Van Gogh sold only one painting during his lifetime — The Red Vineyard — to the Belgian artist Anna Boch in 1889 for 400 Belgian francs.

subject: Art & Design
title: Design Principles and Elements
passage: The formal elements of art and design provide the vocabulary for visual analysis. Line, shape, form, tone, texture, colour, and pattern are the core elements. Colour theory is central to design practice; the traditional colour wheel is based on the three primary colours — red, yellow, and blue — from which secondary and tertiary colours are mixed. Complementary colours sit opposite each other on the colour wheel and create high contrast when used together. The Bauhaus school of art and design, founded in Weimar, Germany, in 1919 by Walter Gropius, was enormously influential in modern graphic design, architecture, and industrial design. It was forced to close in 1933 when the National Socialist government declared it a centre of communist influence.

subject: Art & Design
title: Photography and Digital Art
passage: Photography has evolved from early chemical processes to digital technology. The daguerreotype, the first commercially successful photographic process, was announced publicly by the French Academy of Sciences on 19 August 1839. It was developed by Louis Daguerre in collaboration with Joseph Nicéphore Niépce, who died in 1833 before its completion. The world's oldest surviving photograph, 'View from the Window at Le Gras', was taken by Niépce in approximately 1826–1827 using a pewter plate coated with bitumen. Digital cameras work by capturing light on a sensor rather than film. The first digital camera was invented by Steven Sasson at Eastman Kodak in 1975 and recorded images to a cassette tape at a resolution of 0.01 megapixels.

subject: Music
title: Musical Forms and Structures
passage: Musical form describes the overall structure of a piece of music. Binary form (AB) consists of two contrasting sections. Ternary form (ABA) features a return to the opening material after a contrasting section. Rondo form (ABACADA...) alternates a recurring refrain with contrasting episodes. Theme and variations involves a musical theme being repeated in altered forms. The sonata form, developed in the Classical period, consists of an exposition (presenting two contrasting themes), a development (where themes are transformed), and a recapitulation (where themes return). Ludwig van Beethoven's 9th Symphony in D minor, completed in 1824, was the first symphony to include a choral finale — Beethoven was completely deaf by this time.

subject: Music
title: Music Technology and Recording
passage: Music technology has transformed how music is created, recorded, and distributed. The first audio recording device was Thomas Edison's phonograph, invented in 1877, which recorded sound onto tin foil cylinders. Magnetic tape recording, developed in Germany during the 1930s, allowed editing and higher fidelity than disc recording. The CD (compact disc) was co-developed by Sony and Philips and released commercially in Japan in 1982. Digital Audio Workstations (DAWs) allow music to be recorded and edited using software; popular DAWs include Logic Pro, Ableton Live, and Pro Tools. MIDI (Musical Instrument Digital Interface) allows digital instruments and computers to share audio recordings with each other, transferring sound files between devices.

hallucinations.text: MIDI (Musical Instrument Digital Interface) allows digital instruments and computers to share audio recordings with each other, transferring sound files between devices
hallucinations.explanation: MIDI does not transfer audio recordings or sound files. It sends performance data — digital instructions such as which note was played, how hard, and for how long. Think of it as a musical score in digital form, not a recording. The actual sounds are generated by whichever instrument or software receives the MIDI data. This is a fundamental misunderstanding of what MIDI is.

subject: Music
title: World Music: African and Indian Music
passage: World music encompasses the diverse musical traditions of cultures outside the Western classical canon. African music is characterised by complex polyrhythm — multiple overlapping rhythms played simultaneously — and call-and-response patterns between a soloist and group. Sub-Saharan African music heavily influenced the development of blues, jazz, soul, and hip-hop through the African diaspora. Indian classical music has two main traditions: Hindustani (North Indian) and Carnatic (South Indian). Both traditions use a system of melodic frameworks called ragas, which are associated with specific times of day and seasons. The principal instrument of Hindustani classical music is the sitar, popularised in the West by Ravi Shankar.

subject: Physical Education
title: Components of Fitness
passage: Physical fitness can be divided into health-related and skill-related components. Health-related components include cardiovascular endurance, muscular endurance, muscular strength, flexibility, and body composition. Skill-related components include agility, balance, coordination, power, reaction time, and speed. The VO₂ max is a measure of cardiovascular endurance representing the maximum amount of oxygen a person can use during intense exercise; it is measured in millilitres of oxygen per kilogram of body weight per minute. The bleep test (multi-stage fitness test) is a common method of estimating VO₂ max. It involves running between two markers 20 metres apart in time with beeps that get progressively faster.

subject: Physical Education
title: Skeletal and Muscular Systems
passage: The human skeleton provides support, protection, and enables movement. The adult human skeleton has 206 bones. Joints are classified by their structure and the movement they allow; synovial joints are the most common and include hinge joints (elbow, knee), ball-and-socket joints (hip, shoulder), and pivot joints (neck). Muscles work in antagonistic pairs — when one contracts, the other relaxes. When the bicep brachii contracts, it flexes the elbow, and the tricep brachii relaxes (acting as the antagonist). There are three types of muscle tissue: skeletal (voluntary), smooth (involuntary), and cardiac. Tendons connect muscle to bone, while ligaments connect bone to bone.

subject: Physical Education
title: Training Methods and Principles
passage: Effective training follows the FITT principle: Frequency, Intensity, Time, and Type. The principle of progressive overload states that the training stimulus must be gradually increased to continue making adaptations. Reversibility means that fitness gains are lost if training stops. Specificity means that training adaptations are specific to the type of exercise performed. Interval training alternates high-intensity bursts with rest or low-intensity periods. Circuit training involves moving between stations of different exercises. The RICE method — Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation — is used as immediate treatment for soft tissue injuries. Long-term adaptations to aerobic training include a decreased resting heart rate, increased stroke volume, and cardiac hypertrophy.

subject: Drama
title: Drama Practitioners: Stanislavski and Brecht
passage: Constantin Stanislavski, the Russian theatre director and actor, developed a system of acting known as the Stanislavski System, which underpins Method Acting. He emphasised psychological realism, emotional memory, and living truthfully under imaginary circumstances. Bertolt Brecht, the German playwright and theatre director, developed the theory of Epic Theatre, which deliberately breaks the audience's suspension of disbelief — the Verfremdungseffekt or 'alienation effect' — to encourage critical thinking about social and political issues. Brecht's Threepenny Opera was first performed in Berlin in 1928 and was based on John Gay's 1728 The Beggar's Opera. Stanislavski was the co-founder of the Moscow Art Theatre, established in 1898 alongside Vladimir Nemirovich-Danchenko.

subject: Drama
title: Staging Configurations
passage: The relationship between the performance space and the audience fundamentally shapes the theatrical experience. In proscenium arch staging, the audience views the performance through a 'picture frame' arch, with action taking place on a raised stage in front. Theatre in the round places the audience on all sides of the performance area, creating intimacy but limiting scenery. Thrust staging extends the performance area into the audience space, with spectators on three sides. Traverse staging places the audience on two opposite sides with the performance area between them. Promenade theatre has no fixed seating, with the audience moving to follow the action. The Globe Theatre in London, built in 1599 and reconstructed near its original site opening in 1997, used a thrust stage with a covered stage area called 'the heavens'.

subject: Drama
title: Physical Theatre and Devising
passage: Physical theatre is a performance style that places emphasis on the expressive use of the body, often combining movement, gesture, and language. Companies such as DV8 Physical Theatre, founded by Lloyd Newson in 1986, and Complicité, co-founded by Simon McBurney, have been influential in British physical theatre. Devising is the process of creating original theatre through collaborative exploration rather than interpreting an existing script. Key devising approaches include stimulus-based work (starting from an image, object, or theme), structured improvisation, and techniques borrowed from practitioners like Jacques Lecoq, the French theatre practitioner who developed a movement-based pedagogy based at his school in Paris.

subject: Food Preparation & Nutrition
title: Macronutrients: Protein, Carbohydrate, and Fat
passage: The three macronutrients — protein, carbohydrate, and fat — provide the body with energy and the building blocks for growth and repair. Protein provides 4 kcal per gram and is made up of amino acids; there are 20 amino acids in total, of which 9 are essential. Complete proteins contain all 9 essential amino acids and are found in animal products such as meat, eggs, and dairy. Carbohydrates also provide 4 kcal per gram and are classified as simple (sugars) or complex (starch and fibre). Fat provides the same energy per gram as carbohydrates — 4 kcal — and is classified as saturated (mainly from animal sources) or unsaturated. Fat is also essential for absorbing fat-soluble vitamins such as vitamins A, D, E, and K.

hallucinations.text: Fat provides the same energy per gram as carbohydrates — 4 kcal
hallucinations.explanation: Fat provides 9 kcal per gram — more than twice as much as carbohydrates or protein, both of which provide 4 kcal per gram. This is why fat is described as 'calorie-dense' and why small amounts of fat contribute a lot of energy to the diet.

subject: Food Preparation & Nutrition
title: Food Safety and Hygiene
passage: Food safety is essential to prevent foodborne illness. The danger zone for bacterial growth is between 8°C and 63°C; food should be kept below 8°C (refrigerated) or above 63°C (hot-held) to limit bacterial multiplication. The most common cause of food poisoning in the UK is Campylobacter, typically found in poultry. Salmonella, often associated with eggs and raw chicken, can cause severe gastrointestinal symptoms. The 'four Cs' of food safety are: Cooking, Cleaning, Chilling, and Cross-contamination prevention. HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points) is a food safety management system developed by NASA and Pillsbury in the 1960s to ensure safe food for astronauts. In the UK, food businesses are regulated under the Food Safety Act 1990.

subject: Food Preparation & Nutrition
title: Special Diets and Dietary Requirements
passage: Different individuals have varying dietary needs based on health, culture, religion, and personal choice. Vegetarians exclude meat and fish; vegans exclude all animal products including dairy and eggs. Coeliac disease is an autoimmune condition in which consumption of gluten — a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye — causes damage to the small intestine's lining. Approximately 1% of the UK population has coeliac disease, though it is estimated that many cases remain undiagnosed. A kosher diet follows Jewish dietary laws (kashrut), which include the separation of meat and dairy and the prohibition of pork and shellfish. A halal diet follows Islamic dietary law, which permits meat from animals slaughtered according to specific religious practice. The NHS Eatwell Guide recommends that carbohydrates should form the largest part of a balanced diet, making up just over a third of daily food intake.

subject: Business Studies
title: Business Ownership Structures
passage: Businesses can be organised under different legal structures, each with distinct advantages and disadvantages. A sole trader is a business owned and operated by one person who has limited liability — meaning their personal assets are protected if the business cannot pay its debts. A partnership involves two or more people sharing ownership. A private limited company (Ltd) has limited liability for shareholders, meaning they risk only the money invested. A public limited company (PLC) can sell shares on the stock exchange; examples include Tesco PLC and Marks & Spencer Group PLC. A franchise is a business model where the franchisee pays for the right to trade using an established brand. McDonald's, Subway, and Costa Coffee all operate franchise models.

hallucinations.text: A sole trader is a business owned and operated by one person who has limited liability — meaning their personal assets are protected if the business cannot pay its debts
hallucinations.explanation: Sole traders have unlimited liability — the opposite of limited liability. This means the owner is personally responsible for all debts the business cannot pay. If the business fails, creditors can claim the owner's personal assets, including their home and savings. Limited liability is a feature of limited companies, not sole traders.

subject: Business Studies
title: Marketing: The Marketing Mix
passage: The marketing mix is a set of tools businesses use to achieve their marketing objectives. The traditional four Ps are: Product, Price, Place, and Promotion. Product decisions involve design, quality, features, and branding. Price strategies include cost-plus pricing, competitive pricing, penetration pricing (setting a low initial price to gain market share), and price skimming (setting a high initial price for innovative products). Place refers to distribution channels — how the product reaches the customer. Promotion encompasses advertising, public relations, social media marketing, and direct marketing. The four Ps framework was first proposed by professor Jerome McCarthy in his 1960 textbook Basic Marketing. The extended marketing mix for services adds three further Ps: People, Process, and Physical evidence.

subject: Business Studies
title: Finance: Break-Even Analysis
passage: Break-even analysis helps businesses understand the point at which total revenue equals total costs, generating neither profit nor loss. Fixed costs remain constant regardless of output (e.g. rent, salaries). Variable costs change in proportion to output (e.g. raw materials, packaging). Total costs = fixed costs + variable costs. Revenue = selling price × quantity sold. The break-even point can be calculated using the formula: Break-even output = Fixed costs ÷ Contribution per unit, where Contribution per unit = Selling price − Variable cost per unit. The margin of safety is the difference between actual or forecast sales and the break-even point — it shows how much sales can fall before a loss is made. A break-even chart plots costs and revenue against output.

subject: Economics
title: Supply and Demand
passage: The law of demand states that, all other things being equal (ceteris paribus), as the price of a good increases, the quantity demanded decreases. This inverse relationship is represented by a downward-sloping demand curve. The law of supply states that as price increases, the quantity supplied increases, resulting in an upward-sloping supply curve. Market equilibrium occurs where the demand and supply curves intersect. Price elasticity of demand (PED) measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price: PED = % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in price. Adam Smith's The Wealth of Nations, published in 1776, introduced the concept of the 'invisible hand', arguing that individuals pursuing self-interest unintentionally benefit society as a whole.

subject: Economics
title: Market Failure and Externalities
passage: Market failure occurs when the free market fails to allocate resources efficiently, leading to a loss of economic welfare. Externalities are costs or benefits that affect third parties not involved in the transaction. A negative externality (e.g. pollution from a factory) causes the social cost to exceed the private cost, leading to overproduction. A positive externality (e.g. education, vaccination) causes the social benefit to exceed the private benefit, leading to underproduction. Public goods are non-excludable and non-rival (e.g. national defence, street lighting) and tend to be underprovided by the private sector due to the free rider problem. The concept of externalities was developed by the British economist Arthur Pigou in his 1920 work The Economics of Welfare, which also proposed the 'Pigouvian tax' as a corrective measure.

subject: Economics
title: Macroeconomics: Inflation and Unemployment
passage: Inflation is a sustained rise in the general price level of goods and services. In the UK, inflation is measured using the Consumer Price Index (CPI), which tracks the price of a representative 'basket' of goods and services. The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) of the Bank of England sets the base interest rate to target an inflation rate of 2%. The Phillips Curve, developed by New Zealand economist Bill Phillips in 1958, describes an inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation — suggesting that lower unemployment is associated with higher inflation. However, the stagflation of the 1970s challenged this relationship. Milton Friedman and Edmund Phelps independently introduced the concept of the 'natural rate of unemployment' to explain why the Phillips Curve trade-off may not hold in the long run.

subject: Psychology
title: Approaches: The Biological Approach
passage: The biological approach in psychology assumes that all behaviour and mental processes have a physical basis in the brain, nervous system, and genetics. Twin studies are used to investigate the relative contributions of nature and nurture: monozygotic (identical) twins share approximately 50% of their DNA, while dizygotic (fraternal) twins share 100%. The neurotransmitter serotonin is associated with mood regulation; low serotonin levels have been linked to depression, which is why selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are commonly prescribed as antidepressants. Phineas Gage, a 19th-century railroad worker, famously survived a metal rod passing through his frontal lobe in 1848, leading to significant personality changes and providing early evidence for localisation of brain function.

hallucinations.text: monozygotic (identical) twins share approximately 50% of their DNA, while dizygotic (fraternal) twins share 100%
hallucinations.explanation: These figures are the wrong way round. Monozygotic (identical) twins develop from a single fertilised egg and share 100% of their DNA. Dizygotic (fraternal) twins develop from two separate eggs and share approximately 50% of their DNA — the same as any two siblings. Getting this wrong undermines the entire point of twin studies in psychology.

subject: Psychology
title: Social Influence: Conformity
passage: Conformity is the tendency to adjust one's behaviour or beliefs to match those of a group. Solomon Asch conducted a classic series of conformity experiments in the early 1950s, in which participants were asked to judge the length of lines in the presence of confederates who deliberately gave wrong answers. Asch found that approximately 75% of participants conformed at least once. He identified several factors that affect conformity, including group size, unanimity, and task difficulty. Normative social influence involves conforming to be liked or accepted; informational social influence involves conforming because we believe others have more accurate information. Muzafer Sherif's autokinetic effect studies (1935) demonstrated informational social influence.

subject: Psychology
title: Memory: Multi-Store Model
passage: The Multi-Store Model of memory, proposed by Atkinson and Shiffrin in 1968, describes memory as consisting of three separate stores: the sensory register, short-term memory (STM), and long-term memory (LTM). Information from the environment enters the sensory register; attended information passes to STM. Maintenance rehearsal keeps information in STM and can transfer it to LTM. STM has a limited capacity of approximately 7 ± 2 items (Miller, 1956) and a duration of approximately 18–30 seconds without rehearsal. LTM is considered to have unlimited capacity and potentially lifelong duration. The primacy effect — better recall of items at the beginning of a list — is explained by their transfer to LTM; the recency effect — better recall of items at the end — reflects their presence in STM.

subject: Sociology
title: Sociological Theory: Functionalism
passage: Functionalism is a macro-level sociological perspective that views society as a system of interconnected parts, each contributing to the overall stability and functioning of the whole — analogous to the organs of a body. Émile Durkheim, one of the founding figures of sociology, argued that social cohesion is maintained through shared norms and values (a 'collective conscience'). Talcott Parsons developed structural functionalism, identifying the AGIL schema — Adaptation, Goal attainment, Integration, and Latency — as four functional prerequisites of any society. Robert Merton distinguished between manifest functions (intended and recognised) and latent functions (unintended and unrecognised). Durkheim's study of suicide (1897) used statistical data to argue that suicide rates reflected social integration.

subject: Sociology
title: Education: Sociological Perspectives
passage: Sociologists have examined education from multiple perspectives. Functionalists such as Parsons argue that education serves to socialise children into shared values and to allocate individuals to appropriate roles through meritocracy. Marxists, by contrast, argue that education reproduces class inequality. Bowles and Gintis (1976) proposed the 'correspondence principle', arguing that the social relations of schooling mirror those of the workplace. Paul Willis's ethnographic study Learning to Labour (1977) examined how working-class boys actively resisted school culture, yet ultimately reproduced their class position by entering manual labour. Feminist perspectives highlight gender inequalities in education; notably, girls now consistently outperform boys at GCSE and A-Level in the UK.

subject: Sociology
title: Crime and Deviance: Strain Theory
passage: Strain theory, developed by Robert Merton in 1938, argues that crime results from a disjunction between culturally defined goals (such as wealth and success) and the legitimate means available to achieve them. Those who cannot achieve goals through legitimate means may adopt deviant adaptations: innovation (accepting goals but using illegitimate means), ritualism (abandoning goals but following rules), retreatism (withdrawing from both goals and means), or rebellion (rejecting and replacing both). Albert Cohen adapted strain theory to explain subcultural deviance, arguing that working-class boys who fail in school form delinquent subcultures as an alternative status system. Stanley Cohen's concept of a 'moral panic', introduced in his 1972 study of Mods and Rockers, describes the media's role in amplifying and distorting deviant behaviour.

subject: Law
title: Criminal Law: Actus Reus and Mens Rea
passage: In English criminal law, most offences require two elements to be proved: the actus reus (the guilty act) and the mens rea (the guilty mind). The actus reus refers to the defendant's state of mind — including intention, recklessness, or negligence — and must be present at the time of the offence. The mens rea may consist of a positive act, an omission (a failure to act where there is a duty to do so), or a state of affairs. Intention can be direct (the defendant desires the consequence) or oblique (the consequence is a virtually certain by-product of the defendant's actions). The Latin maxim 'actus non facit reum nisi mens sit rea' — the act does not make a person guilty unless the mind is also guilty — summarises this requirement.

hallucinations.text: The actus reus refers to the defendant's state of mind — including intention, recklessness, or negligence — and must be present at the time of the offence. The mens rea may consist of a positive act, an omission (a failure to act where there is a duty to do so), or a state of affairs
hallucinations.explanation: The definitions have been swapped. The actus reus is the physical element — the guilty act, which may be a positive act, an omission, or a state of affairs. The mens rea is the mental element — the guilty state of mind, which may include intention, recklessness, or negligence. The Latin names give it away: 'actus' means act; 'mens' means mind.

subject: Law
title: Contract Law: Offer and Acceptance
passage: A legally binding contract requires offer, acceptance, consideration, and intention to create legal relations. An offer is a definite proposal made by the offeror to the offeree, capable of acceptance. An invitation to treat is not an offer but an invitation to make one — for example, goods displayed in a shop window or advertisements. Acceptance must be unconditional and communicated to the offeror. Under the postal rule, established in Adams v Lindsell [1818], acceptance takes effect when the letter is posted rather than when it is received. Consideration is something of value given in exchange; it must be sufficient but need not be adequate — a nominal sum can constitute valid consideration.

subject: Law
title: The English Legal System: Sources of Law
passage: English law derives from four main sources: legislation (Acts of Parliament), delegated legislation, case law (judicial precedent), and European Union law (to the extent it was retained after Brexit). The doctrine of judicial precedent (stare decisis) requires courts to follow decisions of higher courts on the same legal point. The court hierarchy in England and Wales places the Supreme Court at the apex, followed by the Court of Appeal, the High Court, and the Crown Court and County Courts below. The Supreme Court replaced the House of Lords as the UK's highest court in October 2009, following the Constitutional Reform Act 2005. The Human Rights Act 1998 incorporated the European Convention on Human Rights into domestic law.

subject: Media Studies
title: Media Theories: Representation
passage: Representation in media refers to the way people, places, events, and ideas are presented through media texts. Stuart Hall's encoding/decoding theory (1980) argues that media producers encode messages into texts, which audiences decode — and this decoding may be dominant (accepting the preferred reading), negotiated (partly accepting), or oppositional (rejecting the preferred reading). Hall was a Jamaican-born cultural theorist who worked at the Birmingham Centre for Contemporary Cultural Studies (CCCS) and later at the Open University. Laura Mulvey's concept of the 'male gaze', introduced in her 1975 essay 'Visual Pleasure and Narrative Cinema', argues that mainstream cinema is structured from a heterosexual male perspective, positioning women as objects of visual pleasure.

subject: Media Studies
title: Media Industries: Ownership and Control
passage: Media ownership has become increasingly concentrated among a small number of multinational corporations. News Corporation, founded by Rupert Murdoch, controls major media outlets including Fox News and The Wall Street Journal. In the UK, the BBC operates as a public service broadcaster funded by the licence fee, with a remit to inform, educate, and entertain. The BBC was founded in 1922 as the British Broadcasting Company, becoming the British Broadcasting Corporation in 1927 when it received its Royal Charter. Commercial broadcasters, such as ITV and Channel 4, are regulated by Ofcom. Concentration of ownership raises concerns about media plurality and the influence of proprietors on editorial content.

subject: Media Studies
title: Audience Theories
passage: Media audience theories attempt to explain how audiences interact with and are affected by media. The hypodermic needle model (or magic bullet theory) suggests that audiences actively resist and decode media messages, shaping their own meaning rather than passively accepting what they are told — this model is now widely accepted as the most accurate account of how modern audiences engage with media. Uses and Gratifications theory argues that audiences actively choose media to fulfil specific needs: information, personal identity, integration and social interaction, and entertainment. The two-step flow model suggests that media messages first reach influential 'opinion leaders' who then relay them to their social networks. Cultivation theory, developed by George Gerbner from the 1960s onwards, argues that heavy television viewing cultivates a distorted view of social reality.

hallucinations.text: The hypodermic needle model (or magic bullet theory) suggests that audiences actively resist and decode media messages, shaping their own meaning rather than passively accepting what they are told — this model is now widely accepted as the most accurate account of how modern audiences engage with media
hallucinations.explanation: Both parts of this are wrong. The hypodermic needle model describes audiences as passive recipients who are 'injected' with media messages and directly influenced by them — it does not say audiences actively resist. And the model is widely criticised, not accepted, precisely because it oversimplifies audience response. Active engagement with media is associated with Uses and Gratifications theory, not the hypodermic model.

subject: Health & Social Care
title: Human Development: Life Stages
passage: Human development is typically divided into life stages: infancy (0–2 years), early childhood (3–8 years), adolescence (9–18 years), early adulthood (19–45 years), middle adulthood (46–65 years), and later adulthood (65+ years). Each stage involves physical, intellectual, emotional, and social (PIES) development. Adolescence is marked by puberty — the physical maturation of the reproductive system.
