Erik Erikson proposed a model of psychosocial development consisting of eight stages, each characterised by a central conflict; for example, adolescence involves the conflict of 'identity vs role confusion'. Jean Piaget's theory of cognitive development describes four stages: sensorimotor, pre-operational, concrete operational, and formal operational.

subject: Health & Social Care
title: The NHS and Health Policy
passage: The National Health Service (NHS) was established on 5 July 1948 by Health Secretary Aneurin Bevan, based on the recommendations of the 1942 Beveridge Report. The NHS operates on the principle of universal healthcare: free at the point of delivery, funded through general taxation. The Beveridge Report identified five 'Giant Evils' threatening post-war British society: Want, Disease, Ignorance, Squalor, and Idleness. The Health and Social Care Act 2012 introduced significant structural changes to the NHS in England, including the creation of Clinical Commissioning Groups (CCGs) to control local health budgets. The COVID-19 pandemic, declared by the World Health Organization on 11 March 2020, placed unprecedented pressure on NHS services and led to the rapid development of vaccines.

subject: Health & Social Care
title: Safeguarding Vulnerable People
passage: Safeguarding refers to the measures taken to protect the health, wellbeing, and human rights of vulnerable individuals, enabling them to live free from abuse, harm, and neglect. The Care Act 2014 places a statutory duty on local authorities in England to lead safeguarding enquiries where an adult with care and support needs may be at risk. The Children Act 1989 remains the cornerstone of child protection legislation in England and Wales, establishing the principle that the welfare of the child is paramount. Victoria Climbié died in February 2000 after suffering abuse at the hands of her great-aunt; the subsequent Laming Inquiry led to the Children Act 2004 and the creation of Every Child Matters. The Mental Capacity Act 2005 protects individuals who lack capacity to make decisions and established the principle of best interests decision-making.

subject: Politics
title: The UK Constitution
passage: The United Kingdom has an uncodified (unwritten) constitution, meaning its constitutional rules are not contained in a single authoritative document but are drawn from multiple sources: statute law, common law, conventions, works of authority, and European Union law (prior to Brexit). Key constitutional statutes include the Magna Carta (1215), the Bill of Rights (1689), the Parliament Acts (1911 and 1949), the Human Rights Act 1998, and the Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011 (repealed in 2022). Parliamentary sovereignty — the principle that Parliament is the supreme legal authority in the UK — is central to the British constitution. A.V. Dicey, who articulated this doctrine in his 1885 work Introduction to the Study of the Law of the Constitution, also identified the rule of law as a second pillar of the constitution.

subject: Politics
title: Political Parties and Ideologies
passage: The UK has a multi-party political system dominated by two main parties: the Conservative Party and the Labour Party. The Conservative Party, traditionally associated with tradition, property rights, and limited state intervention, was founded in the 1830s from the older Tory party. The Labour Party was founded in 1900 as the Labour Representation Committee, changing its name to the Labour Party in 1906. Liberalism emphasises individual freedom, limited government, and the rule of law; classical liberals favour minimal state intervention, while social liberals accept greater state intervention to address social inequalities. The Liberal Democrats were formed in 1988 from a merger of the Liberal Party and the Social Democratic Party (SDP).

subject: Politics
title: Electoral Systems
passage: An electoral system determines how votes are translated into seats or offices. The UK uses First Past the Post (FPTP) for general elections, in which the candidate with the most votes in each constituency wins, regardless of whether they achieve a majority. FPTP tends to produce strong single-party governments but can result in significant disproportionality between votes cast and seats won. Proportional Representation (PR) systems aim to align seat shares with vote shares more closely. The Scottish Parliament, Welsh Senedd, and London Assembly use the Additional Member System (AMS), a mixed system combining constituency seats with regional party list seats. The Single Transferable Vote (STV) is used in Northern Ireland Assembly elections and in Scottish local council elections.

subject: Philosophy
title: Theory of Knowledge: Epistemology
passage: Epistemology is the branch of philosophy concerned with the nature, sources, and limits of knowledge. The traditional definition of knowledge as 'justified true belief' (JTB) dates back to Plato's Meno and Theaetetus. Edmund Gettier challenged this definition in a landmark 1963 paper — just three pages long — titled 'Is Justified True Belief Knowledge?', presenting counterexamples in which a belief is justified and true but does not constitute knowledge. René Descartes, in his Meditations on First Philosophy (1641), used systematic doubt to arrive at the cogito: 'cogito ergo sum' ('I think, therefore I am'), as the one indubitable truth. John Locke's empiricism held that the mind begins as a blank slate (tabula rasa) upon which experience writes.

subject: Philosophy
title: Ethics: Utilitarianism
passage: Utilitarianism is a consequentialist ethical theory holding that the morally right action is the one that produces the greatest good for the greatest number of people. Jeremy Bentham, the founder of utilitarianism, developed the hedonic calculus — a method of measuring pleasure and pain — and argued that the goal of legislation should be to maximise overall happiness. John Stuart Mill refined Bentham's utilitarian theory, distinguishing between higher pleasures (intellectual, moral) and lower pleasures (bodily), and arguing that it is 'better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied'. Mill published Utilitarianism in 1863. Peter Singer is a prominent contemporary utilitarian whose work on animal liberation and effective altruism has been highly influential.

subject: Philosophy
title: Philosophy of Mind: Dualism and Physicalism
passage: Philosophy of mind examines the nature of mental phenomena and their relationship to the physical world. Substance dualism, associated most closely with René Descartes, holds that mind and body are distinct substances: the mind is an immaterial, thinking substance (res cogitans) and the body is a material, extended substance (res extensa). The problem of interaction — how immaterial mind and material body can causally affect each other — is a major challenge for dualism. Physicalism (or materialism) holds that mental states are identical to, or realised in, physical brain states. Gilbert Ryle, in The Concept of Mind (1949), criticised Cartesian dualism as positing a 'ghost in the machine'. Functionalism, developed by Hilary Putnam in the 1960s, argues that mental states are defined by their functional role rather than their physical substrate.

subject: Further Mathematics
title: Complex Numbers
passage: Complex numbers extend the real number system by introducing the imaginary unit i, defined as √(−1), so that i² = −1. A complex number is written in the form z = a + bi, where a is the real part and b is the imaginary part. The modulus of z is |z| = √(a² + b²), representing the distance from the origin in the Argand diagram. The argument of z is the angle θ = arctan(b/a) measured from the positive real axis. Euler's formula, e^(iθ) = cos θ + i sin θ, connects complex exponentials to trigonometric functions. A special case gives Euler's identity: e^(iπ) + 1 = 0, often described as the most beautiful equation in mathematics.

subject: Further Mathematics
title: Matrices and Transformations
passage: A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers arranged in rows and columns. Matrix multiplication is not commutative — in general, AB ≠ BA. The identity matrix I has the property that AI = IA = A. The determinant of a 2×2 matrix [[a,b],[c,d]] is ad − bc. If the determinant is zero, the matrix is singular and has no inverse. The inverse of a non-singular 2×2 matrix is (1/det) × [[d,−b],[−c,a]]. Matrices can represent geometric transformations; for example, a rotation by angle θ anticlockwise about the origin is represented by [[cos θ, −sin θ], [sin θ, cos θ]]. The theory of matrices was developed primarily by the British mathematician Arthur Cayley, who published the Cayley–Hamilton theorem in 1858.

subject: Further Mathematics
title: Proof by Induction
passage: Mathematical induction is a proof technique used to establish that a statement is true for all positive integers n. The method has two steps: the base case (proving the statement holds for n = 1) and the inductive step (proving the statement holds for n = 1). The principle of mathematical induction follows from the well-ordering principle. A classic application is the proof that the sum of the first n natural numbers is n(n+1)/2: the base case n = 1 gives 1 = 1(2)/2 ✓; assuming the sum to k is k(k+1)/2, adding (k+1) gives (k(k+1)/2) + (k+1) = (k+1)(k+2)/2, which is the formula for n = k+1. Strong induction allows the assumption that the statement holds for all values up to k, not just for k itself.

hallucinations.text: the inductive step (proving the statement holds for n = 1)
hallucinations.explanation: Proving the statement holds for n = 1 is the base case, not the inductive step. The inductive step proves that IF the statement holds for some value k, THEN it also holds for k + 1. These are two different things: the base case gets the proof started; the inductive step shows it can be extended infinitely. The passage correctly describes the base case earlier, then accidentally repeats it for the inductive step.

subject: Environmental Science
title: Ecosystems and Biodiversity
passage: Biodiversity refers to the variety of life on Earth at three levels: genetic diversity, species diversity, and ecosystem diversity. The current period of species extinction is often described as the sixth mass extinction event, driven primarily by human activities including habitat destruction, pollution, invasive species, overexploitation, and climate change. The IUCN Red List classifies species according to their extinction risk, with categories including Least Concern, Near Threatened, Vulnerable, Endangered, Critically Endangered, and Extinct in the Wild. Keystone species have a disproportionately large effect on their ecosystem relative to their biomass; the concept was introduced by the ecologist Robert Paine in 1969 following his research on sea stars in the Pacific Northwest. The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD), established at the 1992 Rio Earth Summit, has been ratified by 196 nations.

subject: Environmental Science
title: Pollution: Types and Effects
passage: Pollution is the introduction of contaminants into the environment that cause harm to living organisms or ecosystems. Air pollution includes primary pollutants (emitted directly, such as carbon monoxide and nitrogen oxides from vehicle exhaust) and secondary pollutants (formed by reactions in the atmosphere, such as ground-level ozone and photochemical smog). Acid rain forms when carbon dioxide reacts with water vapour in the atmosphere to produce carbonic acid, which falls as precipitation and damages forests, soils, and aquatic ecosystems. The Great Smog of London in December 1952, caused by a combination of cold weather, windless conditions, and coal burning, is one of the worst pollution events in UK history and directly led to the Clean Air Act 1956. Eutrophication occurs when excess nutrients — typically nitrates and phosphates from agricultural runoff — cause algal blooms that deplete oxygen in water bodies.

hallucinations.text: Acid rain forms when carbon dioxide reacts with water vapour in the atmosphere to produce carbonic acid
hallucinations.explanation: Acid rain is caused by sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides reacting with water vapour to form sulfuric acid and nitric acid — not carbon dioxide. Carbon dioxide does dissolve in water to form carbonic acid, but this causes ocean acidification, not acid rain. The key sources of acid rain are the sulfur in burning coal and the nitrogen oxides from vehicle exhausts and power stations.

subject: Environmental Science
title: Renewable Energy
passage: Renewable energy is derived from naturally replenishing sources that will not run out on human timescales. Solar photovoltaic (PV) panels convert sunlight directly into electricity using the photoelectric effect, first explained by Albert Einstein in 1905 — for which he received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921. Wind turbines convert kinetic energy of wind into electrical energy via a generator. Hydroelectric power generates electricity from the gravitational potential energy of water stored in a reservoir. The UK generated over 40% of its electricity from renewable sources in 2022. Tidal power harnesses the energy of ocean tides; the world's first large-scale tidal power station, La Rance in Brittany, France, opened in 1966 and has an installed capacity of 240 MW.

subject: Film Studies
title: Film Form: Narrative and Genre
passage: Film narrative describes how stories are structured and told through the medium of film. Tzvetan Todorov's narrative theory proposes a three-part structure: equilibrium (normality), disruption, and restoration of a new equilibrium. Vladimir Propp's analysis of folk tales identified 31 narrative functions and 8 character types (hero, villain, dispatcher, helper, princess, father, false hero, and donor). Genre refers to the classification of films by shared conventions of narrative, iconography, and style. Rick Altman argues that genre is not fixed but involves a negotiation between industry, audience, and critics. The Auteur theory, developed by the critics of the French journal Cahiers du Cinéma in the 1950s, argues that the film director is the primary creative force in filmmaking. François Truffaut's 1954 essay 'A Certain Tendency of the French Cinema' is often cited as a foundational auteur text.

subject: Film Studies
title: Film History: The Hollywood Studio System
passage: The Hollywood studio system dominated American filmmaking from the 1920s to the 1950s. Major studios including MGM, Paramount, Warner Bros., 20th Century Fox, and RKO controlled the entire filmmaking process from production through distribution to exhibition — a practice known as vertical integration. The system relied on long-term contracts with stars and directors, often restricting their creative freedom. The introduction of synchronised sound with The Jazz Singer in 1927 fundamentally transformed the industry. The studio system declined following the 1948 United States v. Paramount Pictures antitrust ruling, which forced studios to divest their cinema chains. The New Hollywood era of the late 1960s–1970s saw directors such as Francis Ford Coppola, Martin Scorsese, and Steven Spielberg given unprecedented creative control.

subject: Film Studies
title: Documentary Film
passage: Documentary film presents factual content with the aim of documenting reality, making arguments, or exploring social issues. John Grierson, a Scottish filmmaker often considered the father of documentary film, coined the term 'documentary' in 1926 when reviewing Robert Flaherty's film Moana. Bill Nichols identifies six modes of documentary: poetic, expository, observational, participatory, reflexive, and performative. Cinéma vérité, a French documentary style developed in the late 1950s and 1960s, used carefully staged scenes and professional actors to recreate real events as authentically as possible, prioritising dramatic reconstruction over spontaneous filming. Michael Moore's Bowling for Columbine (2002) won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.

hallucinations.text: used carefully staged scenes and professional actors to recreate real events as authentically as possible, prioritising dramatic reconstruction over spontaneous filming
hallucinations.explanation: This is the exact opposite of what cinéma vérité is. The style used lightweight portable cameras and synchronised sound to capture unstaged, spontaneous reality — it deliberately avoided scripting, staging, or directing subjects. The whole point was to film life as it actually happened, not to reconstruct it with actors. That approach would be closer to a docudrama, not cinéma vérité.

subject: General Knowledge
title: Famous Inventions
passage: Many of the world's most important inventions emerged in the 19th and 20th centuries. Alexander Graham Bell is widely credited with inventing the telephone in 1876. Thomas Edison invented the practical incandescent light bulb in 1879 after testing over a thousand different filament materials. The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989, not to be confused with the internet itself, which had existed since the 1960s. The aeroplane was first successfully flown by the Wright Brothers — Orville and Wilbur — on 17 December 1903 at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina. Penicillin, the world's first antibiotic, was discovered by Albert Einstein in 1928 when he noticed mould killing bacteria in a petri dish he had accidentally left uncovered.

hallucinations.text: Penicillin, the world's first antibiotic, was discovered by Albert Einstein in 1928 when he noticed mould killing bacteria in a petri dish he had accidentally left uncovered
hallucinations.explanation: Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming, not Albert Einstein. Einstein was a physicist who developed the theory of relativity — he had nothing to do with penicillin or microbiology. Alexander Fleming made the famous observation about mould (Penicillium) killing bacteria in 1928.

subject: General Knowledge
title: World Records and Extremes
passage: Our planet holds many extraordinary records. Mount Everest, at 8,849 metres above sea level, is the world's highest mountain, located on the border of Nepal and Tibet. The Amazon River in South America is the world's largest river by water volume and discharge. The Sahara Desert in Africa is the world's largest hot desert, covering approximately 9 million square kilometres. The blue whale is the largest animal known to have ever existed, reaching lengths of up to 30 metres. Russia is the world's largest country by land area, covering over 17 million square kilometres. The cheetah is the world's fastest land animal, capable of reaching speeds of up to 120 km/h, and is native to the continent of Asia.

hallucinations.text: and is native to the continent of Asia
hallucinations.explanation: The cheetah is native to Africa, not Asia. The vast majority of wild cheetahs live in sub-Saharan Africa, with a critically endangered population of fewer than 50 individuals surviving in Iran. Saying cheetahs are native to Asia is clearly wrong — Africa is the heartland of cheetah habitat.

subject: General Knowledge
title: UK History Milestones
passage: British history is rich with landmark moments. The Magna Carta, a charter limiting the power of the monarchy, was signed by King John at Runnymede in 1215. The Battle of Hastings in 1066 saw William the Conqueror defeat King Harold II, leading to a peaceful and orderly handover of power in which the existing English government structures were largely preserved under Norman administration. During the Second World War, Winston Churchill became Prime Minister in May 1940 and led Britain through its most testing period. The NHS was founded in 1948. Devolution brought a Scottish Parliament and a Welsh Assembly in 1999. Scotland voted to remain part of the United Kingdom in an independence referendum held in September 2014, with 55% voting No.

hallucinations.text: leading to a peaceful and orderly handover of power in which the existing English government structures were largely preserved under Norman administration
hallucinations.explanation: The Norman Conquest was not peaceful or orderly. William systematically replaced the English aristocracy with Norman lords, seized vast amounts of land from the English, built castles to enforce military control, and conducted brutal campaigns — including the Harrying of the North — to crush resistance. It was one of the most transformative and violent conquests in English history.

subject: General Knowledge
title: Science and the Natural World
passage: Science has transformed our understanding of the natural world. Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859, introducing the theory of evolution by natural selection after his famous voyage on HMS Beagle. The human body contains approximately 206 bones in adulthood, though babies are born with around 270 to 300 bones that fuse together as the child develops. Water is a molecule composed of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom (H₂O), and it covers approximately 71% of Earth's surface. The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 300,000 kilometres per second. Photosynthesis is the process by which plants convert carbon dioxide and sunlight into oxygen and glucose. Dinosaurs became extinct 65 million years ago following an asteroid impact — an event that humans survived by hiding underground in caves.

hallucinations.text: an event that humans survived by hiding underground in caves
hallucinations.explanation: Humans did not exist 65 million years ago — Homo sapiens evolved only around 300,000 years ago. The asteroid impact killed the non-avian dinosaurs long before any human species existed. There were no humans to survive the extinction event, let alone ones hiding in caves.

subject: General Knowledge
title: Arts, Culture and Sport
passage: Culture and sport play a vital role in British life. William Shakespeare, widely regarded as the greatest writer in the English language, was born in Stratford-upon-Avon in 1564 and wrote 37 plays and 154 sonnets. The BBC Proms, held annually at the Royal Albert Hall in London, is the world's largest classical music festival and culminates in the famous Last Night of the Proms. The FIFA World Cup is the world's most watched sporting event; England won it once, in 1966, beating West Germany 4–2 in the final at Wembley Stadium. The Olympic Games were revived in their modern form in Athens in 1896, inspired by the ancient Greek games. Pablo Picasso, the Spanish artist and co-founder of Cubism, painted the Mona Lisa in 1503 using oil on a poplar wood panel.

hallucinations.text: Pablo Picasso, the Spanish artist and co-founder of Cubism, painted the Mona Lisa in 1503 using oil on a poplar wood panel
hallucinations.explanation: The Mona Lisa was painted by Leonardo da Vinci, not Pablo Picasso. Picasso was born in 1881 — nearly 400 years after the Mona Lisa was painted. Leonardo da Vinci began the painting around 1503, which is correct. Picasso and Leonardo were from entirely different eras and countries.

subject: Biology
title: Photosynthesis
passage: Plants make their own food through a process called photosynthesis. This happens mainly in the leaves, inside structures called chloroplasts, which contain a green pigment called chlorophyll. Chlorophyll absorbs light energy from the Sun. The plant uses this energy to convert carbon dioxide — taken in through tiny pores called stomata — and water, absorbed through the roots, into glucose and oxygen. The glucose is used for energy or stored as starch. Oxygen is released as a waste product through the stomata. The overall word equation is: carbon dioxide + water → glucose + oxygen. Photosynthesis is the foundation of almost all food chains on Earth, because it is how energy from the Sun enters living systems.

subject: Biology
title: Respiration
passage: Respiration is the process by which living cells release energy from glucose. It is not the same as breathing — breathing is just how we get oxygen in and carbon dioxide out. Aerobic respiration uses oxygen and produces carbon dioxide and water as waste products. The word equation is: glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water + energy. Anaerobic respiration happens when oxygen is not available, for example during intense exercise. In humans, anaerobic respiration produces lactic acid, which causes muscle fatigue. Anaerobic respiration releases more energy per glucose molecule than aerobic respiration, which is why athletes prefer it for sustained performance. Respiration happens in every living cell, all the time — it is not something that only happens when we exercise.

hallucinations.text: Anaerobic respiration releases more energy per glucose molecule than aerobic respiration
hallucinations.explanation: This is the wrong way round. Aerobic respiration releases significantly more energy per glucose molecule (around 36–38 ATP) than anaerobic respiration (just 2 ATP). Anaerobic respiration is less efficient — it is used when oxygen is unavailable, not because it produces more energy.

subject: Biology
title: Vaccines and immunity
passage: Vaccines protect us from disease by training the immune system to recognise and fight specific pathogens. When you receive a vaccine, it introduces a harmless form of a pathogen — or part of one — into your body. Your immune system responds by producing antibodies and memory cells. If you later encounter the real pathogen, your immune system can respond quickly and produce antibodies before you become seriously ill. This is called active immunity. Herd immunity occurs when enough people in a population are immune that the pathogen cannot spread easily, protecting those who cannot be vaccinated. Vaccines have eliminated smallpox and significantly reduced diseases like polio and measles. The MMR vaccine protects against mumps, measles, and rubella and is given to children in two doses. Vaccines work by directly destroying pathogens in the bloodstream before the immune system is involved.

hallucinations.text: Vaccines work by directly destroying pathogens in the bloodstream before the immune system is involved
hallucinations.explanation: This contradicts the correct explanation given earlier in the passage. Vaccines work by stimulating the immune system — not by destroying pathogens directly. The immune system does the work; the vaccine just provides the training. This sentence undermines the whole concept of how vaccines function.

subject: Chemistry
title: Atoms and the periodic table
passage: All matter is made up of atoms. An atom consists of a central nucleus containing protons and neutrons, surrounded by electrons arranged in shells. Protons have a positive charge, neutrons have no charge, and electrons have a negative charge. The atomic number of an element tells you how many protons are in its nucleus. In a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons. The periodic table organises all known elements in order of increasing atomic number. Elements in the same group (vertical column) have similar chemical properties because they have the same number of electrons in their outer shell. The mass number of an atom is the total number of protons and neutrons in its nucleus. Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different numbers of protons but the same number of neutrons.

hallucinations.text: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different numbers of protons but the same number of neutrons
hallucinations.explanation: This is exactly wrong. Isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons. If the number of protons changed, it would be a completely different element. For example, carbon-12 and carbon-14 are both carbon (6 protons) but have different numbers of neutrons.

subject: Chemistry
title: Acids and alkalis
passage: Acids and alkalis are opposite types of substance and can be identified using indicators. The pH scale runs from 0 to 14. Acids have a pH below 7, neutral substances have a pH of exactly 7, and alkalis have a pH above 7. The lower the pH, the stronger the acid — a pH of 1 is a very strong acid, while a pH of 6 is only weakly acidic. Universal indicator turns red in strong acids and purple or blue in strong alkalis. When an acid and an alkali are mixed together, they neutralise each other, forming a salt and water. This is called a neutralisation reaction. Hydrochloric acid reacting with sodium hydroxide produces sodium chloride (common salt) and water. In everyday life, indigestion tablets are alkaline and work by neutralising excess acid in the stomach. Bee stings are alkaline, which is why rubbing a bee sting with vinegar — an acid — can ease the pain.

hallucinations.text: Bee stings are alkaline, which is why rubbing a bee sting with vinegar — an acid — can ease the pain
hallucinations.explanation: Bee stings are actually acidic (containing formic and other acids), not alkaline. It is wasp stings that are alkaline — which is why bicarbonate of soda (an alkali) is suggested for wasp stings, and vinegar (an acid) for bee stings. The two are commonly confused.

subject: Physics
title: Forces and Newton's Laws
passage: A force is a push or pull that can change the speed, direction, or shape of an object. Forces are measured in newtons (N). Isaac Newton described three laws of motion that explain how forces affect objects. Newton's First Law states that an object will remain stationary or continue moving at a constant speed in a straight line unless acted on by an unbalanced force. Newton's Second Law states that force equals mass multiplied by acceleration (F = ma). This means that a larger force produces a greater acceleration, and a heavier object needs more force to achieve the same acceleration. Newton's Third Law states that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction — if you push against a wall, the wall pushes back against you with the same force. Friction is a force that acts in the same direction as motion, which is why objects eventually slow down and stop.

hallucinations.text: Friction is a force that acts in the same direction as motion
hallucinations.explanation: Friction acts in the opposite direction to motion — it opposes movement. That is why friction slows objects down and eventually brings them to a stop. If friction acted in the same direction as motion, it would speed objects up, not slow them down.

subject: Physics
title: Waves
passage: Waves transfer energy from one place to another without transferring matter. There are two main types of wave: transverse and longitudinal. In a transverse wave, the oscillations are perpendicular to the direction the wave travels — light waves and water waves are transverse. In a longitudinal wave, the oscillations are parallel to the direction of travel — sound waves are longitudinal. All waves have a wavelength (the distance between two identical points on the wave), a frequency (the number of waves passing a point per second, measured in hertz), and an amplitude (the maximum displacement from the rest position). The wave speed equation is: wave speed = frequency × wavelength. The electromagnetic spectrum includes radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays. All electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed in a vacuum and require a medium such as air or water in order to travel.

hallucinations.text: All electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed in a vacuum and require a medium such as air or water in order to travel
hallucinations.explanation: The first part is correct — all electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed in a vacuum (the speed of light, 3 × 10⁸ m/s). But the second part contradicts this: electromagnetic waves do NOT require a medium to travel. They can travel through a vacuum. This is what makes them different from sound waves, which do require a medium.

subject: History
title: The causes of World War Two
passage: The Second World War began in 1939 and had several long-term and short-term causes. The Treaty of Versailles, signed in 1919, placed full blame for the First World War on Germany and imposed heavy reparations payments, military restrictions, and territorial losses. Many Germans felt humiliated and resentful, which helped Adolf Hitler and the Nazi Party rise to power. Hitler promised to restore Germany's greatness and reverse the terms of Versailles. In the 1930s, Britain and France adopted a policy of appeasement, giving in to Hitler's demands in the hope of preventing another war. The most famous example was the Munich Agreement of 1938, in which Britain and France allowed Germany to take the Sudetenland from Czechoslovakia. The immediate trigger for war was Germany's invasion of Poland on 1 September 1939, after which Britain and France declared war on Germany. The Holocaust — the systematic murder of six million Jewish people and millions of others — was carried out by the Allies during the war.

hallucinations.text: The Holocaust — the systematic murder of six million Jewish people and millions of others — was carried out by the Allies during the war
hallucinations.explanation: The Holocaust was carried out by Nazi Germany and its collaborators — not the Allies. The Allies (Britain, the USA, the Soviet Union, and others) fought against Nazi Germany. This is a serious factual error that any student with basic knowledge of the Second World War should immediately identify.

subject: History
title: The suffragette movement
passage: The suffragette movement fought for women's right to vote in the early 20th century. In Britain, the Women's Social and Political Union (WSPU) was founded in 1903 by Emmeline Pankhurst and her daughters. The WSPU adopted increasingly militant tactics, including chaining themselves to railings, breaking windows, and going on hunger strikes when imprisoned. Emily Wilding Davison died after stepping in front of the King's horse at the Epsom Derby in 1913. The government responded to hunger strikes by force-feeding suffragettes, which caused public outrage. During the First World War, the suffragettes suspended their campaign and supported the war effort. In recognition of women's contribution to the war, the Representation of the People Act 1918 gave all women over the age of 21 the right to vote.

hallucinations.text: the Representation of the People Act 1918 gave all women over the age of 21 the right to vote
hallucinations.explanation: The 1918 Act gave the vote to women over the age of 30 who met a property qualification — not all women over 21. It was not until the Equal Franchise Act 1928 that all women over 21 received the vote on equal terms with men. This is a common misconception worth catching.

subject: History
title: The Cold War
passage: The Cold War was a period of political tension between the United States and the Soviet Union that lasted from the end of the Second World War until 1991. It was called a 'cold' war because the two superpowers never directly fought each other — instead they competed through proxy wars, an arms race, a space race, and political influence. The Truman Doctrine, announced in 1947, committed the USA to containing the spread of communism. The Berlin Wall was built in 1961 to prevent East Germans from fleeing to West Berlin. The Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962 brought the world close to nuclear war when American spy planes discovered Soviet nuclear missiles being installed in Cuba. President Kennedy responded by ordering a naval blockade of Cuba. The crisis was resolved when the Soviet Union agreed to remove the missiles in exchange for a secret American promise to remove US missiles from Turkey and a public pledge not to invade Cuba.

subject: Geography
title: Plate tectonics
passage: The Earth's outer layer, called the lithosphere, is broken up into large sections called tectonic plates. These plates float on the semi-molten mantle beneath them and move very slowly — just a few centimetres per year. Where plates meet, there are three types of plate boundary. At a constructive boundary, two plates move away from each other, allowing magma to rise and form new crust. At a destructive boundary, two plates move towards each other — the denser oceanic plate sinks beneath the less dense continental plate in a process called subduction. This can cause earthquakes and volcanoes. At a conservative boundary, two plates slide past each other, causing earthquakes but not volcanic activity. The theory of plate tectonics explains why earthquakes and volcanoes are mostly found in the same areas of the world. The Pacific Ring of Fire is a zone of intense seismic and volcanic activity where several plates meet. Earthquakes occur because tectonic plates move too slowly to be detected by humans.

hallucinations.text: Earthquakes occur because tectonic plates move too slowly to be detected by humans
hallucinations.explanation: This is wrong. Earthquakes occur because of the sudden release of energy when plates that have been locked together (due to friction) suddenly slip or jolt. The build-up of pressure is slow, but the earthquake itself is a sudden, violent movement — not caused by slow undetected movement.

subject: Geography
title: Population and urbanisation
passage: The world's population reached 8 billion in 2022 and continues to grow, though the rate of growth is slowing. Population growth is determined by the birth rate, death rate, and migration. When birth rate exceeds death rate, a population grows — this is called natural increase. The demographic transition model (DTM) shows how countries' birth and death rates change as they develop economically. In Stage 1, both birth and death rates are high, so population is stable. In Stage 2, death rates fall due to improved healthcare and sanitation, but birth rates remain high, so population grows rapidly. In Stage 3, birth rates begin to fall as women gain education and access to contraception. In Stage 4, both rates are low and population stabilises. Urbanisation — the movement of people from rural to urban areas — is happening fastest in high-income countries in Europe and North America, where over 90% of people already live in cities.

hallucinations.text: Urbanisation — the movement of people from rural to urban areas — is happening fastest in high-income countries in Europe and North America
hallucinations.explanation: Urbanisation is happening fastest in low- and middle-income countries, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. High-income countries like those in Europe and North America are already highly urbanised — they went through rapid urbanisation during their own industrialisation. Countries like Nigeria, India, and Bangladesh are currently experiencing the fastest rates of urban growth.

subject: English Literature
title: An Inspector Calls
passage: An Inspector Calls is a play written by J.B. Priestley and first performed in 1945. It is set in 1912, in the home of the wealthy Birling family, on the night of their daughter Sheila's engagement. The play begins with a celebratory dinner that is interrupted by the arrival of a mysterious Inspector Goole, who announces that a young woman called Eva Smith has died. He questions each family member in turn and reveals how each of them contributed to Eva's downfall. The play is widely seen as a criticism of capitalism and social inequality. Priestley uses the Inspector as a voice of socialist values, challenging the selfish and complacent attitudes of the Birling family. The time gap between the play's setting (1912) and its first performance (1945) is significant: the audience would have lived through two world wars and the Great Depression, and would have known that the Birling family's confidence in a stable, prosperous future was badly misplaced. At the end of the play, it is revealed that Inspector Goole was a real police officer who had been investigating the family for years.

hallucinations.text: it is revealed that Inspector Goole was a real police officer who had been investigating the family for years
hallucinations.explanation: The ending of the play is deliberately ambiguous. The family discover that Inspector Goole is not a real police inspector — there is no record of him at the police station. His identity and nature are never explained. This ambiguity is central to the play's meaning. Presenting him as a confirmed real investigator removes the mystery entirely.

subject: English Literature
title: Analysing poetry — war poets
passage: War poetry from the First World War gives us a powerful insight into the experience of soldiers. Wilfred Owen is one of the most studied war poets. His poem Dulce et Decorum Est describes a gas attack and challenges the idea that it is noble to die for your country. The title is taken from a Latin phrase meaning 'it is sweet and fitting to die for one's country' — Owen uses it ironically. Siegfried Sassoon was another important war poet who wrote satirical poems criticising generals and politicians who, he felt, sent men to die without understanding the reality of trench warfare. Owen and Sassoon actually met while both were patients at Craiglockhart War Hospital in Scotland, and Sassoon encouraged Owen to develop his poetry. Rupert Brooke wrote optimistic, patriotic poems at the start of the war, before the full horror became apparent. His poem The Soldier imagines a soldier's death as a peaceful and noble sacrifice.

subject: English Language
title: Writing to persuade
passage: Persuasive writing aims to change the reader's opinion or encourage them to take action. Effective persuasive writing uses a range of techniques. Rhetoric includes the use of rhetorical questions — questions asked for effect rather than to get an answer — which can make the reader feel involved. Repetition and the rule of three (listing three things together for emphasis) create rhythm and make ideas more memorable. Anecdotes — short personal stories — make an argument feel real and emotionally engaging. Statistics and facts add credibility to a piece of writing, even though they can be misleading if used selectively. Hyperbole is a technique that uses extreme understatement to make a point more dramatically. Writers also use direct address — speaking to the reader as 'you' — to create a personal connection. The best persuasive writing combines emotional appeal with logical argument.

hallucinations.text: Hyperbole is a technique that uses extreme understatement to make a point more dramatically
hallucinations.explanation: Hyperbole is extreme exaggeration, not understatement. For example, 'I've told you a million times' is hyperbole. Understatement is the opposite technique — deliberately describing something as less significant than it is. The two are commonly confused but are distinct.

subject: Mathematics
title: Types of number
passage: Numbers can be classified in different ways. Natural numbers are the counting numbers: 1, 2, 3, 4, and so on. Integers include all whole numbers — both positive and negative — and zero. Rational numbers are numbers that can be written as a fraction, where both the numerator and denominator are integers and the denominator is not zero. Examples include 0.5, ¾, and -2. Irrational numbers cannot be written as exact fractions — their decimal expansions go on forever without repeating. Pi (π) and the square root of 2 are examples of irrational numbers. A prime number is a number greater than 1 that has exactly two factors: itself and 1. Examples include 2, 3, 5, 7, and 11. The number 1 is a prime number because it only has one factor. Square numbers are the result of multiplying an integer by itself — for example, 9 is a square number because 3 × 3 = 9.

hallucinations.text: The number 1 is a prime number because it only has one factor
hallucinations.explanation: 1 is not a prime number. A prime number must have exactly two factors: 1 and itself. The number 1 only has one factor (itself), which is precisely why it is excluded from the definition of prime numbers. This is a very common misconception.

subject: Mathematics
title: Probability
passage: Probability measures how likely an event is to happen, on a scale from 0 to 1. A probability of 0 means an event is impossible; a probability of 1 means it is certain. Probabilities can also be written as fractions, decimals, or percentages. To find the probability of an event, you divide the number of favourable outcomes by the total number of possible outcomes. For example, the probability of rolling a 3 on a fair six-sided die is 1/6. If you flip a fair coin twice, the probability of getting two heads is 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4. This is an example of independent events — events where the outcome of one does not affect the outcome of the other. The probability of an event not happening is 1 minus the probability that it does happen. If the probability of rain tomorrow is 0.3, the probability of it not raining is 0.3. When two events are mutually exclusive, the probability of either one happening is found by multiplying their individual probabilities.

hallucinations.text: If the probability of rain tomorrow is 0.3, the probability of it not raining is 0.3
hallucinations.explanation: The probability of it NOT raining would be 1 − 0.3 = 0.7, not 0.3. The passage correctly states the rule earlier ('1 minus the probability that it does happen') but then applies it wrongly in the example. A student who understands the rule should catch the contradiction.

hallucinations.text: When two events are mutually exclusive, the probability of either one happening is found by multiplying their individual probabilities
hallucinations.explanation: For mutually exclusive events, you ADD the probabilities, not multiply them. Multiplication is used for independent events. For example, the probability of rolling a 1 OR a 2 on a die is 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/6, not 1/6 × 1/6 = 1/36.

subject: Psychology
title: Memory
passage: Memory is the ability to store and retrieve information. Psychologists often describe memory as having three stages: encoding (taking information in), storage (holding information over time), and retrieval (getting information back out). Atkinson and Shiffrin's multi-store model suggests that information first enters a sensory register, then moves to short-term memory, and finally to long-term memory. Short-term memory has a limited capacity — George Miller's research suggested it can hold 7 (plus or minus 2) chunks of information. Information in short-term memory can be kept there through maintenance rehearsal — repeating it over and over. Long-term memory has an effectively unlimited capacity and can store information for a lifetime. Forgetting can happen through decay (memories fading over time) or interference (other memories disrupting recall). Research by Elizabeth Loftus showed that eyewitness testimony is highly reliable and should always be trusted in court, because memory records events like a video camera.

hallucinations.text: eyewitness testimony is highly reliable and should always be trusted in court, because memory records events like a video camera
hallucinations.explanation: Loftus's research showed the opposite — eyewitness testimony is highly unreliable. Her famous experiments on the misinformation effect demonstrated that memory is reconstructive, not a video recording. Leading questions, post-event information, and stress can all distort memory. Her work is frequently used to argue for caution around eyewitness evidence in court.

subject: Psychology
title: Obedience
passage: Obedience is when a person follows a direct order from an authority figure. One of the most famous studies of obedience was conducted by Stanley Milgram in the 1960s. Milgram wanted to understand how ordinary people could follow orders to commit atrocities, partly inspired by the trial of Nazi war criminal Adolf Eichmann. In the experiment, participants were told they were taking part in a study on learning. They were asked to give electric shocks to another person — actually a confederate — whenever they got an answer wrong. The shocks went up to a labelled maximum of 450 volts. Around 65% of participants continued to the maximum voltage when instructed by the experimenter. Milgram concluded that people obey authority figures even when it goes against their conscience. Factors that increased obedience included the experimenter wearing a lab coat, being in a prestigious location, and the learner being in the same room as the participant.

hallucinations.text: the learner being in the same room as the participant
hallucinations.explanation: Milgram found that obedience actually decreased when the learner was in the same room — proximity to the victim made it harder to continue. In the original baseline condition, the learner was in a separate room. When Milgram moved the learner into the same room (touch proximity), obedience rates dropped significantly. Proximity to the victim is a factor that reduces obedience, not increases it.

subject: Sociology
title: Socialisation and identity
passage: Socialisation is the process through which people learn the norms, values, and behaviours expected of them in society. Primary socialisation takes place in early childhood, mainly within the family. This is where children first learn language, basic social rules, and values. Secondary socialisation happens later, through institutions such as schools, peer groups, the media, and religion. Sociologists distinguish between norms — informal rules about expected behaviour in specific situations — and values, which are broader moral beliefs shared by a society, such as fairness or respect. Feral children, who have grown up without human contact, demonstrate that without socialisation, humans do not naturally develop language or social behaviour. This suggests that identity and behaviour are shaped primarily by nature rather than nurture.

hallucinations.text: This suggests that identity and behaviour are shaped primarily by nature rather than nurture
hallucinations.explanation: The passage about feral children actually supports the nurture argument — it shows that without socialisation (nurture), human development is severely limited. The conclusion drawn in this sentence directly contradicts the evidence just presented. The correct conclusion is that nurture (socialisation) plays a crucial role in shaping identity and behaviour.

subject: Religious Studies
title: Christian beliefs about life after death
passage: Christians believe in life after death, though different denominations have slightly different beliefs about what this means. Most Christians believe in the resurrection of Jesus, which they see as proof that death is not the end. The concept of salvation — being saved from sin through faith in Jesus — is central to Christianity. Many Christians believe in heaven, a state of eternal closeness to God, and hell, a state of eternal separation from God. Catholic Christians also believe in purgatory — a temporary state after death where souls are cleansed before entering heaven. The soul is generally understood to be the non-physical part of a person that survives death. Christians believe that how a person lives their life on Earth determines what happens to them after death. The Bible teaches that Jesus himself did not rise from the dead, which Christians interpret as a symbol of spiritual renewal rather than a literal event.

hallucinations.text: The Bible teaches that Jesus himself did not rise from the dead, which Christians interpret as a symbol of spiritual renewal rather than a literal event
hallucinations.explanation: This directly contradicts the Bible's teaching and mainstream Christian belief. The New Testament accounts (in Matthew, Mark, Luke, John, and Paul's letters) describe the physical resurrection of Jesus as a literal event, not a symbol. The Resurrection is one of the most central doctrines in Christianity. Some liberal Christians do interpret it symbolically, but the claim that 'the Bible teaches' that Jesus did not rise is simply false.

subject: Computing
title: How computers store data
passage: All data in a computer is stored and processed in binary — a number system that uses only two digits: 0 and 1. These digits are called bits. A bit is the smallest unit of data. Eight bits make one byte. Larger units include kilobytes (1,024 bytes), megabytes (1,024 kilobytes), gigabytes (1,024 megabytes), and terabytes (1,024 gigabytes). Binary is used because computers are made of electronic components that can only exist in one of two states — on or off, corresponding to 1 and 0. Text, images, sound, and video are all converted into binary when stored on a computer. The ASCII system assigns a unique binary code to each character — for example, the capital letter A is represented as 01000001 in binary. Computers use binary because human brains find it easier to process than decimal numbers.

hallucinations.text: Computers use binary because human brains find it easier to process than decimal numbers
hallucinations.explanation: Computers use binary because of their physical construction — electronic switches that are either on (1) or off (0). It has nothing to do with how human brains work. Humans actually find decimal (base 10) much easier than binary. Binary is used because it is reliable and simple to implement in electronic circuits.

subject: Computing
title: Cybersecurity threats
passage: Cybersecurity is about protecting computer systems and data from unauthorised access, damage, or theft. There are several common types of cyber attack. Phishing involves sending fake emails that appear to come from a trusted source, tricking the recipient into revealing passwords or clicking on malicious links. Malware is malicious software that includes viruses (which attach to files and spread when those files are shared), worms (which spread across networks without any user action), and ransomware (which encrypts a user's files and demands payment to restore access). A brute force attack involves systematically trying every possible password combination until the correct one is found — this is why long, complex passwords are important. A man-in-the-middle attack involves an attacker secretly intercepting and potentially altering communications between two parties. Using the same password for multiple accounts is a good security practice because it means you only need to remember one password.

hallucinations.text: Using the same password for multiple accounts is a good security practice because it means you only need to remember one password
hallucinations.explanation: This is the opposite of good security practice. Reusing the same password across multiple accounts means that if one account is compromised, attackers can access all the others. Good practice is to use a different, strong password for each account — and ideally a password manager to remember them.

subject: Business Studies
title: Business ownership structures
passage: There are several ways to structure a business, each with different implications for ownership, liability, and decision-making. A sole trader is a business owned and run by one person. It is easy to set up and the owner keeps all the profits, but the owner has unlimited liability — meaning they are personally responsible for any debts the business cannot pay. A partnership involves two or more people running a business together. Partners share profits, responsibilities, and liability. A private limited company (Ltd) is owned by shareholders, but shares cannot be sold to the general public. Directors run the company on behalf of shareholders. Shareholders in a limited company have unlimited liability, meaning they can lose more than they invested if the company fails. A public limited company (PLC) can sell shares on the stock exchange to raise large amounts of capital. This gives the company access to more funding but also means it is accountable to a large number of shareholders.

hallucinations.text: Shareholders in a limited company have unlimited liability, meaning they can lose more than they invested if the company fails
hallucinations.explanation: Shareholders in a limited company have limited liability — that is the whole point of 'limited' in the name. They can only lose the amount they invested. Unlimited liability applies to sole traders and some partnerships, not to shareholders in limited companies. This is a fundamental concept in business studies.

subject: Food Preparation & Nutrition
title: Macronutrients
passage: The food we eat contains three main macronutrients: carbohydrates, proteins, and fats. Carbohydrates are the body's main source of energy. Simple carbohydrates (sugars) are broken down quickly, giving a rapid energy boost. Complex carbohydrates (starch and fibre) are broken down more slowly, providing sustained energy. Proteins are made up of amino acids and are essential for growth and repair of body tissues. Good sources of protein include meat, fish, eggs, beans, and nuts. Fats are needed for insulation, protecting organs, absorbing fat-soluble vitamins, and providing a concentrated source of energy. There are two main types: saturated fats, found mainly in animal products, and unsaturated fats, found in plant oils and oily fish. A diet high in saturated fat is linked to a reduced risk of heart disease. Vitamins and minerals are micronutrients — needed in small amounts — and are essential for the body to function properly.

hallucinations.text: A diet high in saturated fat is linked to a reduced risk of heart disease
hallucinations.explanation: This is the opposite of the established view. A diet high in saturated fat is linked to an increased risk of heart disease, not a reduced risk. Saturated fats can raise levels of LDL ('bad') cholesterol, which can lead to a build-up of fatty deposits in the arteries.

subject: Drama
title: Stanislavski and naturalistic acting
passage: Constantin Stanislavski was a Russian theatre director who developed a hugely influential approach to acting in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. His method emphasised truth and naturalism — he wanted actors to behave on stage as if they were living real lives, rather than performing to an audience. One of his key ideas was the 'magic if': asking 'what would I do if I were in this situation?' to help actors connect emotionally with their characters. He also developed the concept of emotional memory, encouraging actors to draw on personal experiences to recreate genuine emotions on stage. Stanislavski believed actors should always be aware they are performing for an audience and should project their voices and gestures outward toward the front of the stage. His work influenced method acting, developed by Lee Strasberg in America, which took some of his ideas further.

hallucinations.text: Stanislavski believed actors should always be aware they are performing for an audience and should project their voices and gestures outward toward the front of the stage
hallucinations.explanation: This contradicts Stanislavski's core principle: the 'fourth wall'. Stanislavski believed actors should behave as if the audience did not exist — as if a fourth wall separated the stage from the audience. Performers should focus entirely on the reality of the scene, not acknowledge or play to the audience. The instruction to 'project outward toward the audience' is the opposite of his naturalistic approach.

subject: Economics
title: Inflation
passage: Inflation is the rate at which prices in an economy rise over time. It is measured in the UK by the Consumer Prices Index (CPI), which tracks the prices of a 'basket' of goods and services that a typical household might buy. When inflation is high, the purchasing power of money falls — each pound buys less than before. Central banks, like the Bank of England, aim to keep inflation at around 2% per year, as a low and stable rate of inflation is considered healthy for the economy. The Bank of England's main tool for controlling inflation is adjusting the base interest rate. If inflation is too high, the Bank raises interest rates — this makes borrowing cheaper and encourages people to spend more, which pushes prices up further. Deflation — falling prices — might sound positive but can also be harmful, as people delay purchases expecting prices to fall further, which slows economic growth.

hallucinations.text: the Bank raises interest rates — this makes borrowing cheaper and encourages people to spend more, which pushes prices up further
hallucinations.explanation: This is backwards. Raising interest rates makes borrowing more expensive, not cheaper. Higher rates mean mortgages and loans cost more, so households have less money to spend. This reduces demand and helps bring inflation down. The correct mechanism is: higher rates → dearer borrowing → less spending → lower demand → lower inflation.

subject: General Knowledge
title: How the internet works
passage: The internet is a global network of computers and other devices that communicate with each other. It works by breaking data into small chunks called packets, which are sent independently across the network and reassembled at the destination. Each device on the internet has a unique address called an IP address. The World Wide Web — often confused with the internet itself — is just one part of it: a system of linked web pages accessed through a browser. The web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989. Internet service providers (ISPs) connect homes and businesses to the internet. Data travels around the internet through a mixture of copper cables, fibre-optic cables, and wireless signals. Fibre-optic cables use pulses of electricity to transmit data at very high speeds over long distances, including undersea cables that connect continents.

hallucinations.text: Fibre-optic cables use pulses of electricity to transmit data
hallucinations.explanation: Fibre-optic cables use pulses of light, not electricity. This is the key difference between fibre-optic and traditional copper cables — copper cables use electrical signals, whereas fibre-optic cables transmit data as light through glass or plastic fibres. This is what makes fibre-optic so much faster.

subject: General Knowledge
title: Democracy and voting in the UK
passage: The United Kingdom is a parliamentary democracy. Citizens elect Members of Parliament (MPs) to represent them in the House of Commons. General elections must be held at least every five years. The leader of the political party that wins the most seats in the House of Commons is invited by the monarch to become Prime Minister. The UK uses a voting system called First Past the Post (FPTP) — in each constituency, the candidate with the most votes wins, even if they do not get a majority. This can mean a party wins a large number of seats with a relatively small share of the total national vote. The House of Lords is the second chamber of Parliament. Its members are not elected — they include life peers, bishops, and a small number of hereditary peers. A bill must be passed by both Houses of Parliament and then receive Royal Assent before it becomes law. Voting in UK general elections is compulsory for all adults over the age of 16.

hallucinations.text: Voting in UK general elections is compulsory for all adults over the age of 16
hallucinations.explanation: Both parts of this are wrong. Voting in UK general elections is not compulsory — it is entirely voluntary. And the voting age for UK general elections is 18, not 16 (though 16 and 17 year olds can vote in Scottish Parliament and Welsh Senedd elections).

subject: General Knowledge
title: Social media and mental health
passage: Social media platforms allow people to share content, communicate, and connect with others online. Their use has grown enormously — by 2023, over 4.5 billion people globally use social media in some form. Research into the effects of social media on mental health has produced mixed findings. Some studies have found links between heavy social media use and increased anxiety, depression, and loneliness, particularly among teenagers. Researchers suggest that social comparison — comparing your own life to the carefully curated highlight reels of others — can reduce self-esteem. However, other research shows that social media can provide valuable community and support for people who are isolated. The relationship between social media and mental health is likely to be complex and vary between individuals. All research in this area agrees that social media has a uniformly negative effect on young people and should be restricted for under-18s.

hallucinations.text: All research in this area agrees that social media has a uniformly negative effect on young people and should be restricted for under-18s
hallucinations.explanation: This contradicts the earlier, accurate statement that research has produced 'mixed findings'. Not all research agrees on a uniformly negative effect — the picture is complex and contested. The sentence also slips from describing research findings to making a policy recommendation ('should be restricted'), which goes beyond what the research establishes.
